That reactive catch — sudden, unexpected load in an awkward position — is one of the highest-injury-risk moments in patient care. Knowing how to recognize early signs of instability in patients you're assisting reduces the frequency of those reactive moments.

Workstation setup for clinical and administrative staff

Extended time at a computer workstation creates cumulative stress on the neck, shoulders, back, and hands. The proper setup:

  • Monitor: Top of screen at or slightly below eye level. Center of screen at roughly chin height when sitting upright. Arms length away (20-24 inches).
  • Keyboard and mouse: Elbows at approximately 90 degrees, wrists neutral (not bent up or down), shoulders relaxed. The keyboard should be flat or slightly negative-tilt — not propped up at the back.
  • Chair: Feet flat on the floor or on a footrest. Thighs roughly parallel to the floor. Lower back supported by the chair's lumbar support or a lumbar cushion. Armrests, if present, at a height that allows shoulders to relax.
  • Exam room workstations: Adjustable-height desks or wall-mounted workstations that can be positioned for both sitting and standing documentation reduce the awkward postures that fixed-height workstations force.

Object lifting — principles that transfer across every situation

  • Plan the lift before you do it. Where does it need to go? Is the path clear? Do you need help?
  • Get close to the load. The further a load is from your body, the more force it places on your spine.
  • Bend your knees and hips — not your waist. Your legs are much stronger than your back.
  • Keep your back in a neutral position — natural curve maintained, not rounded or hyperextended.
  • Never twist while lifting. Turn your feet, not your spine.
  • If it's too heavy or awkward: ask for help or use equipment. Every time.

Micro-breaks and task variation

Repetitive tasks, extended static postures, and sustained force all create cumulative stress on specific tissues. Short, frequent breaks are more effective than longer, infrequent ones. For tasks involving repetitive hand use, a 30-second stretch break every 20-30 minutes is more protective than one 10-minute break every two hours.

Varying tasks — alternating between sitting and standing, between computer work and patient care, between writing and other tasks — reduces prolonged loading on any single set of muscles or joints.

Key takeaways

  • Back injuries accumulate over time. The 35-lb patient handling limit is about what the spine tolerates over a career, not what you can lift once.
  • Use mechanical assists for patient transfers above threshold. "I can handle it" is how careers get shortened.
  • Workstation setup: monitor at eye level, keyboard at elbow height, wrists neutral, feet supported.
  • When lifting: get close, bend knees not waist, neutral spine, no twisting.
  • Short, frequent micro-breaks are more protective than long, infrequent ones for repetitive tasks.

Ready for the quiz. Six questions. You need 80% to pass.

quizQuestions.question: 'Ergonomics' in the workplace refers to:
options: The study of how quickly employees can perform repetitive tasks
The science of designing tasks, tools, and environments to fit the worker and reduce injury risk
The process of measuring employee productivity over time
A form of physical therapy for injured workers
quizQuestions.explanation: Ergonomics is the science of fitting the job to the worker — designing tasks, tools, equipment, and environments to minimize physical strain and reduce the risk of musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs).

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a musculoskeletal risk factor during clinical tasks?
options: Using a height-adjustable exam table when positioning patients
Holding awkward postures (reaching, twisting, bending) for extended periods
Using wheeled equipment to move patients between rooms
Wearing slip-resistant footwear during patient transfers
quizQuestions.explanation: Musculoskeletal risk factors include: awkward postures (reaching above shoulder height, bending forward, twisting), forceful exertions, repetitive motions, contact stress, and static postures. Using adjustable equipment and wheeled devices are preventive measures.

quizQuestions.question: Using a mechanical lift for patient transfers primarily reduces the risk of:
options: Patient falls from height
Worker musculoskeletal injuries (especially back injuries) from manual patient handling
Bloodborne pathogen exposure during patient contact
Patient pressure injuries during extended bed rest
quizQuestions.explanation: Mechanical lifts and assistive transfer devices are the single most effective intervention for preventing musculoskeletal injuries from patient handling. Back injuries are the most common and costly occupational injury in healthcare.

quizQuestions.question: When lifting a heavy box from the floor, the correct technique is to:
options: Bend at the waist and use your back muscles to lift
Squat with a straight back, hold the load close to your body, and use your leg muscles to lift
Twist your spine as you lift to get more leverage
Lean forward with a rounded back to get under the load
quizQuestions.explanation: Safe lifting: squat close to the load, keep your back straight (not rounded), hold the load close to your body, use leg muscles to power the lift, and avoid twisting while lifting. Bending at the waist places extreme stress on lumbar discs.

quizQuestions.question: Repetitive motions in healthcare (such as extended typing or repetitive instrument use) primarily increase the risk of:
options: Bloodborne pathogen exposure
Cumulative trauma disorders such as carpal tunnel syndrome and tendinitis
Respiratory infections from airborne pathogens
Fatigue-related medication errors
quizQuestions.explanation: Repetitive motions — especially those combined with force and awkward postures — can lead to cumulative trauma disorders (CTDs) such as carpal tunnel syndrome, tendinitis, and trigger finger. Micro-breaks and varied tasks help prevent CTDs.

quizQuestions.question: The recommended maximum weight for a single healthcare worker to manually handle during patient transfers is:
options: 50 lbs with proper body mechanics
35 lbs — above this, mechanical assistance or additional personnel should be used
75 lbs if the worker has completed ergonomics training
100 lbs using a gait belt
quizQuestions.explanation: NIOSH and safe patient handling guidelines recommend a 35-lb limit for manual patient-handling tasks. Tasks exceeding this threshold should use mechanical lift equipment, assistive devices, or at least two workers to prevent musculoskeletal injuries.

title: New Hire: Practice Policies & Procedures
description: Understand the importance of practice-specific policies, where to find them, your obligations to follow them, and how to report policy concerns.
lessonContent:
Key Terms
HIPAA = Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
OSHA = Occupational Safety and Health Administration
PHI = Protected Health Information
ePHI = Electronic Protected Health Information
OCR = Office for Civil Rights

Before we start — policies aren't bureaucracy, they're evidence

In an OCR investigation or an OSHA inspection, the question isn't just "what happened" — it's "what was your process, and did you follow it?" A practice with documented policies that were followed is in a completely different legal position than a practice that operated on informal habits and assumptions. Policies protect patients by creating consistency. They protect you by establishing clear expectations. And they protect the practice by creating a documented record of how care was intended to be delivered.

Why policies exist — the three real reasons

  • Consistency: Policies ensure that the right things happen regardless of which staff member is on duty, regardless of how busy the day is, regardless of whether the supervisor is in. A practice that depends on individuals always doing the right thing from memory will have variable outcomes. A practice with clear, followed policies has consistent ones.
  • Regulatory compliance: HIPAA requires written policies. OSHA requires written programs (Exposure Control Plan, HazCom program, Emergency Action Plan). Accreditation bodies require documented processes. Many of your practice's policies exist because a law or regulation requires them to.
  • Legal protection: When something goes wrong — and eventually something will — a well-documented policy that was consistently followed is evidence of due diligence. The absence of a policy, or a policy that exists but wasn't followed, is the opposite of that.

Where to find your practice's policies

You should know where policies are located and how to access them before you need one in a specific situation. Ask your supervisor during your first week:

  • Where is the policy system or policy folder?
  • How do you search for a specific policy?
  • How do you know if a policy has been recently updated?
  • Who do you contact if a policy seems unclear or out of date?

Policies are only useful if people can access them. If access is difficult, the practice has a compliance problem — and surfacing that early is genuinely helpful.

What a policy acknowledgment means

When you sign or electronically acknowledge a policy, you are confirming that you have read it, that you understand it, and that you agree to follow it. This is a real commitment — not a box-checking exercise.

If you acknowledge a policy you haven't actually read, and an incident occurs that the policy addressed, the defense "I didn't know" is gone. You signed that you did know.

Read the policies you acknowledge. If something is unclear, ask before you sign — not after something goes wrong.

When to follow a policy and when to question one

Policies should be followed as written. Full stop — with one narrow exception: in a genuine, immediate life-threatening emergency where strict adherence to a policy would cause direct, immediate harm, clinical judgment may override the policy. That situation should be documented immediately after and reported to your supervisor.

Routine inconvenience is not an exception. "The policy takes longer" is not an exception. "The physician prefers a different approach" may mean the policy needs to be updated — but it doesn't mean the policy can be ignored in the meantime.

If you believe a policy is outdated, incorrect, or creates a safety problem: report it through the appropriate channel (your supervisor or the quality/compliance team). Follow the policy while advocating for its revision. That's the right process.

A scenario worth remembering: A staff member believes the practice's patient communication policy is overly restrictive and frequently skips the required steps. A patient complaint arises that directly implicates the communication policy. The practice is now in a position of explaining why their documented policy wasn't followed — which is significantly harder than explaining why the policy is the way it is.

Confidentiality agreements — what you signed means something

Most practices require new employees to sign a confidentiality agreement as part of onboarding. That agreement typically covers:

  • All forms of patient PHI — verbal, written, and electronic
  • Business-sensitive information — financials, strategy, contracts, personnel matters
  • Post-employment obligations — confidentiality doesn't end when your employment does

Violations of a confidentiality agreement can result in disciplinary action up to and including termination, and in some cases personal legal liability. "I didn't think it mattered outside of work" is not a defense.

Policy review and updates

Policies are living documents. HIPAA requires that policies be reviewed and updated regularly — at least annually, and whenever there's a significant change in operations, regulations, or technology. When a policy is updated, you'll typically be notified and asked to re-acknowledge.

When you receive a policy update notification: don't dismiss it. Read what changed. The update exists because something meaningful changed — in the law, in a procedure, or based on a lesson learned from an incident.

Key takeaways

  • Policies create consistency, ensure compliance, and provide legal protection. They exist for real reasons.
  • Know where your practice's policies are and how to access them before you need one.
  • A policy acknowledgment is a real commitment — read before you sign.
  • Follow policies as written. If you disagree with a policy, raise it through proper channels — don't just stop following it.
  • Confidentiality agreements extend beyond employment. What you learned here stays here.

Ready for the quiz. Six questions. You need 80% to pass.

quizQuestions.question: Practice policies and procedures are important because they:
options: Protect the practice from liability but have no direct impact on patient care
Standardize care, ensure regulatory compliance, and reduce errors by providing clear guidance
Are legally required but rarely followed in real clinical practice
Primarily serve as documentation for accreditation surveys
quizQuestions.explanation: Policies and procedures standardize how work is done, help ensure regulatory compliance (HIPAA, OSHA, accreditation), reduce variability and errors, and document the practice's commitment to patient safety and quality care.

quizQuestions.question: When is it appropriate for an employee to deviate from a documented practice policy?
options: When the employee believes a different approach would be faster
In an immediately life-threatening emergency where strict adherence would cause harm, documented promptly after
Whenever a senior colleague suggests a better way
Deviation from policy is never appropriate under any circumstance
quizQuestions.explanation: Policies should be followed as written. In rare life-threatening emergencies where strict adherence would cause immediate harm, deviation may be justified — but must be documented promptly afterward and reported to supervisors. Routine shortcuts or personal preference do not justify deviation.

quizQuestions.question: Where can employees typically find the practice's current policies and procedures?
options: Only in the printed manual kept in the administrator's office
The practice's designated policy management system, shared drive, or intranet — per your orientation
Posted on the practice's public website
In the federal HIPAA and OSHA regulations documents
quizQuestions.explanation: Practices should maintain current policies in a centralized, accessible location (electronic policy management system, shared drive, or intranet). You should know where to find policies and check for the most current version before performing unfamiliar procedures.

quizQuestions.question: If you believe an existing policy is outdated or could be improved, you should:
options: Ignore the policy and follow your own best judgment
Alert all your coworkers to also disregard the policy
Report your concern through the designated policy review process (supervisor or quality committee)
Post about it on your personal social media to raise awareness
quizQuestions.explanation: Policies should be reviewed and updated through a formal process. If you believe a policy is outdated or unsafe, report your concern to your supervisor or the quality/compliance committee. Following the policy while advocating for change through proper channels is the correct approach.

quizQuestions.question: A confidentiality agreement signed during onboarding typically covers:
options: Only electronic patient records — verbal disclosures are not covered
Patient PHI, proprietary business information, and your obligation not to share confidential information after employment ends
Your social media conduct outside of work hours only
Only disclosures that could cause financial harm to the practice
quizQuestions.explanation: Confidentiality agreements typically cover: all forms of PHI (verbal, written, electronic), business information (financial data, strategies, contracts), and post-employment obligations. Violations can result in disciplinary action, termination, and potential legal liability.

quizQuestions.question: Practice policies regarding patient communication through electronic channels (email, text message) are primarily governed by:
options: The practice's marketing policy
HIPAA requirements for the secure transmission of ePHI and patient consent for electronic communications
State insurance regulations
The physician's personal preference for communication methods
quizQuestions.explanation: Electronic communications with patients involving PHI are governed by HIPAA's Security Rule requirements for ePHI in transit. Patients must also consent to receiving PHI via unencrypted channels (email or text) if they choose to accept that risk.

title: Annual OSHA Refresher
description: Review key OSHA compliance obligations, recordkeeping requirements, recent enforcement priorities, and your employees' rights under the OSH Act.
lessonContent:
Key Terms
OSHA = Occupational Safety and Health Administration
BBP = Bloodborne Pathogens
SDS = Safety Data Sheet
PPE = Personal Protective Equipment

Before we start — enforcement is active

OSHA conducted over 32,000 federal inspections in fiscal year 2023, with healthcare among the most frequently inspected sectors. Penalties for willful violations can reach $156,259 per violation. But the more common and more damaging problem is the unaddressed hazard that results in a worker injury — with the workers' comp costs, lost productivity, and potential litigation that follow. Annual refresher training is how you stay current on requirements that change, and how your employer documents that their workforce was trained.

What "recordable" means — and why it matters

Not every workplace injury ends up on the OSHA 300 log — but far more do than most employees realize. A work-related injury or illness is recordable if it results in any of the following:

  • Days away from work
  • Restricted work activity or job transfer
  • Medical treatment beyond first aid
  • Loss of consciousness
  • Significant diagnosed injury or illness, even without the above

First aid — cleaning a wound, applying a bandage, using a non-prescription medication — is not recordable. As soon as care crosses into prescription medication, sutures, physical therapy, or any of the above outcomes, it becomes recordable.

Why it matters to you: if you downplay an injury to avoid it being recorded, and that injury later requires medical treatment, the lack of an initial report creates documentation problems that can affect your workers' compensation claim and the practice's OSHA compliance record.

The Exposure Control Plan — what it is and where to find it

OSHA's Bloodborne Pathogens Standard requires a written Exposure Control Plan (ECP) that documents how the practice protects workers from bloodborne pathogen exposure. It must include:

  • Identification of job classifications with exposure risk
  • The engineering and work practice controls in use
  • The PPE available and when it's required
  • The Hepatitis B vaccination program
  • Post-exposure evaluation and follow-up procedures
  • The sharps injury log and how incidents are documented

The ECP must be reviewed and updated at least annually — and whenever new tasks or procedures are added that affect exposure risk. You should know where the ECP is located. If you don't, ask your supervisor today.

HazCom — the ongoing obligations

The Hazard Communication Standard creates ongoing obligations beyond initial training:

  • SDSs must be maintained for all hazardous chemicals currently in use — and must be accessible during every work shift.
  • The chemical inventory must be current. When new products are introduced, SDSs must be obtained before use.
  • Training must be updated when a new hazard is introduced. A new cleaning product with different hazard characteristics isn't covered by training on the old product.
  • Secondary containers must be labeled — except for immediate same-shift use by the person who filled them.

Recordkeeping deadlines — the calendar you need to know

  • Work-related fatalities: Report to OSHA within 8 hours
  • Hospitalizations, amputations, eye loss: Report to OSHA within 24 hours
  • Recordable injuries and illnesses: Log on OSHA Form 300 within 7 calendar days of learning of the case
  • OSHA 300A Summary posting: February 1 through April 30 every year, covering the prior calendar year, in a visible location in the workplace
  • Records retention: OSHA 300, 300A, and 301 forms must be retained for 5 years

Your anti-retaliation rights — a refresher

Section 11(c) of the OSH Act prohibits employers from retaliating against workers who exercise their OSHA rights. Protected activities include:

  • Reporting a workplace injury or illness
  • Filing a complaint with OSHA
  • Participating in an OSHA inspection
  • Refusing imminent-danger work in good faith
  • Raising safety concerns with your employer

If you believe you've been retaliated against for exercising these rights, you have 30 days to file a whistleblower complaint with OSHA. This protection exists specifically because OSHA needs workers to report hazards accurately, and that only happens if workers feel safe doing so.

Key takeaways

  • Recordable injuries include anything requiring more than first aid. Report all workplace injuries on the day they occur regardless of severity.
  • The Exposure Control Plan must be reviewed annually and updated when procedures change. Know where to find it.
  • HazCom obligations are ongoing: maintain current SDSs, update training when new hazards arrive, keep secondary containers labeled.
  • Key calendar: fatalities within 8 hours, hospitalizations/amputations within 24 hours, recordable cases within 7 days, 300A summary posted Feb 1–Apr 30.
  • You cannot be retaliated against for reporting safety concerns. 30-day window to file a complaint if you are.

Ready for the quiz. Six questions. You need 80% to pass.

quizQuestions.question: OSHA's General Duty Clause (Section 5(a)(1)) requires employers to:
options: Provide workers' compensation insurance for all injuries
Furnish each employee a place of employment free from recognized hazards likely to cause death or serious physical harm
Allow OSHA inspectors unlimited access to all company records
Report all workplace injuries to OSHA within 8 hours
quizQuestions.explanation: The General Duty Clause is a catch-all provision that requires employers to address recognized workplace hazards even when no specific OSHA standard covers the hazard. It is frequently used by OSHA to cite employers for workplace violence, heat illness, and other hazards.

quizQuestions.question: Under OSHA's recordkeeping rule (29 CFR 1904), employers with fewer than how many employees are partially exempt from routine injury recordkeeping?
options: 5 employees
10 employees
25 employees
50 employees
quizQuestions.explanation: Employers with 10 or fewer employees are partially exempt from OSHA's injury and illness recordkeeping requirements (though they must still report fatalities and severe injuries). Most healthcare practices with 11+ employees are subject to full recordkeeping.

quizQuestions.question: The OSHA 300A Summary of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses must be posted in the workplace annually from:
options: January 1 through January 31
February 1 through April 30
March 1 through the end of the calendar year
July 1 through December 31
quizQuestions.explanation: The OSHA 300A Summary must be posted in a visible location from February 1 through April 30 of the year following the recorded events. It must be certified (signed) by an executive or owner of the establishment.

quizQuestions.question: Under the BBP Standard, the Exposure Control Plan must be reviewed and updated:
options: Every 3 years
At least annually and when new tasks creating exposure risk are added
Only after a needlestick injury occurs
When a new OSHA inspector requests a review
quizQuestions.explanation: The Exposure Control Plan must be reviewed and updated at least annually. It must also be updated whenever new or modified tasks or procedures affecting occupational exposure are added, and when new safety-engineered devices are available and considered.

quizQuestions.question: OSHA's whistleblower protection program protects employees who:
options: Report suspected fraud to the IRS
Report workplace safety violations to OSHA or refuse work they reasonably believe poses imminent danger
File grievances with their union
Report harassment to their HR department
quizQuestions.explanation: OSHA's Section 11(c) anti-retaliation provision protects employees who report safety and health concerns to OSHA, refuse to perform work they reasonably believe poses an imminent danger, or participate in OSHA inspections or proceedings.

quizQuestions.question: A work-related amputation (e.g., partial finger amputation from a piece of equipment) must be reported to OSHA within:
options: 8 hours
24 hours
7 calendar days
30 days
quizQuestions.explanation: OSHA requires employers to report in-patient hospitalizations, amputations, and loss of an eye to OSHA within 24 hours. Work-related fatalities must be reported within 8 hours. The OSHA 300 log must be updated within 7 calendar days of learning of a recordable case.

title: Cybersecurity Best Practices for Medical Offices
description: Protect ePHI and practice systems with essential cybersecurity skills: recognizing phishing, password hygiene, ransomware prevention, and safe device use.
lessonContent:
Key Terms
HIPAA = Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
ePHI = Electronic Protected Health Information
MFA = Multi-Factor Authentication
BAA = Business Associate Agreement

Before we start — healthcare is the prime target

A complete medical record sells for up to $1,000 on the dark web — compared to about $5 for a stolen credit card number. Credit cards can be canceled. Medical records contain permanent information — Social Security numbers, insurance details, diagnoses, medications — that can be used for identity theft, insurance fraud, and medical identity theft for years. Healthcare organizations are targeted because the data is valuable and because many organizations have historically underinvested in security. Your individual behavior is a meaningful part of the defense.

The phishing problem — still the most common attack vector

The majority of successful cyberattacks on healthcare organizations begin with a phishing email — an email designed to trick you into clicking a malicious link, opening a malicious attachment, or entering your credentials on a fake website.

Phishing emails have become increasingly sophisticated. They use real company logos, mimic the writing style of colleagues, create urgency ("your account will be suspended in 24 hours"), and often arrive from addresses that look almost right on quick inspection.

Before clicking any link or opening any attachment:

  • Hover over the link and look at where it actually goes. The display text can say anything — what matters is the actual URL.
  • Look at the sender address character by character, not just the display name. "IT Department <itsupport@g00gle.com>" — the display name looks fine; the address is a fake.
  • Ask yourself: was I expecting this? Did I initiate a process that would require this email?
  • When in doubt: call the sender using a known phone number. Not the number in the suspicious email. A number you find independently.
Smishing (SMS phishing): The same tactics applied to text messages. "Your EHR account requires verification — click here." Your IT department does not send credential requests by text message. No legitimate system does.

Password hygiene — the basics that still fail constantly

  • Use a unique password for each system. When one system is breached (and systems get breached), credential stuffing attacks try those credentials on every other system automatically. Reused passwords mean one breach becomes many.
  • Use a password manager. If your practice provides one, use it. A password manager is the only practical way to have strong, unique passwords for every system without writing them on sticky notes.
  • Never share your credentials. Not with your supervisor. Not "just for today." Not so they can access something while you're out. Individual logins exist so individual access can be audited. Shared credentials make the audit trail meaningless.
  • Enable multi-factor authentication (MFA) wherever it's available. MFA means a stolen password alone isn't enough to access your account — the attacker also needs your phone or another second factor.

Ransomware — what it is and what prevents it

Ransomware is malware that encrypts your files and demands payment for the decryption key. Healthcare ransomware attacks have shut down hospital systems for weeks, delayed patient care, and resulted in hundreds of millions of dollars in recovery costs. The FBI generally advises against paying ransoms — it doesn't guarantee data recovery and funds the attackers.

The two best defenses:

  • Not clicking phishing links. Most ransomware enters through email. You are the first line of defense.
  • Tested, current backups. Ransomware is devastating when you have no clean backup to restore from. It's a manageable incident when you do. The practice's backup strategy is largely out of your hands — but your personal behavior on whether you click suspicious links very much is not.

Device security — the basics that matter most

  • Lock your workstation whenever you step away. Windows+L. Command+Control+Q on Mac. Three seconds. Every time.
  • Don't use personal devices for work unless the practice has a documented BYOD policy covering encryption, approved apps, and remote wipe capability.
  • Keep software updated. Updates exist because vulnerabilities were found. "Remind me later" keeps known vulnerabilities open.
  • Don't plug unknown USB drives into practice computers. USB drives are a physical vector for malware delivery.
  • Report lost or stolen devices immediately. Don't wait to see if it turns up. Every hour of delay is an hour the data is potentially accessible.

Common mistakes

  • Clicking "verify your account" emails. If your EHR or any other practice system needs you to verify something, you'll be prompted when you log in — not via an unsolicited email.
  • Using the same password for your work EHR and your personal Gmail. When Gmail gets compromised (and credentials leak constantly), your EHR access is also compromised.
  • Ignoring browser security warnings. If your browser says "this site's security certificate is invalid" or "this connection is not secure" — that's meaningful. Don't click through.

Key takeaways

  • Medical records are worth far more than credit card data on the dark web. You are protecting something highly valuable.
  • Phishing is the most common attack vector. Pause before clicking any link or opening any attachment. When in doubt, call.
  • Unique passwords for every system. Use a password manager. Enable MFA wherever available. Never share credentials.
  • Lock your workstation every time you step away. Three seconds prevents a lot.
  • Report lost or stolen devices immediately — don't wait to see if they turn up.

Ready for the quiz. Seven questions. You need 80% to pass.

quizQuestions.question: Which type of cyberattack has been most frequently associated with large HIPAA breaches in recent years?
options: Physical theft of paper records
Ransomware and hacking of network servers
Employees emailing records to incorrect recipients
Unauthorized access by former employees
quizQuestions.explanation: Ransomware and hacking incidents have accounted for the majority of large HIPAA breaches (those affecting 500+ individuals) in recent years. Cybercriminals target healthcare organizations because PHI has high black-market value.

quizQuestions.question: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) protects your accounts by:
options: Encrypting your password so it cannot be read
Requiring a second form of verification (e.g., a code sent to your phone) in addition to your password
Automatically logging you out after 15 minutes of inactivity
Preventing the use of weak passwords
quizQuestions.explanation: MFA requires users to provide two or more verification factors — something you know (password), something you have (phone/token), or something you are (biometric). Even if a password is compromised, MFA prevents unauthorized access.

quizQuestions.question: You receive a text message claiming to be from IT asking you to click a link and confirm your EHR login credentials. This is most likely:
options: A legitimate IT security audit
A smishing (SMS phishing) attack attempting to steal your credentials
An automated password reset triggered by your account's expiration
A routine system check that all employees receive
quizQuestions.explanation: Smishing (SMS phishing) uses text messages to trick recipients into revealing credentials or clicking malicious links. Legitimate IT departments do not request credentials via text. Report suspicious texts to your IT department immediately.

quizQuestions.question: A HIPAA-compliant approach to sending PHI via email is:
options: Using any email service as long as the subject line does not mention the patient's name
Encrypting the email or using a secure messaging platform with a signed BAA
Sending PHI only to other healthcare providers since they are also covered entities
Adding 'CONFIDENTIAL' to the subject line as the security measure
quizQuestions.explanation: Unencrypted email is not HIPAA-compliant for transmitting PHI. Compliant options include: end-to-end encrypted email services with a signed BAA, secure patient portal messaging, or obtaining explicit patient consent to use unencrypted email with acknowledgment of the risk.

quizQuestions.question: Regular, tested data backups are the primary defense against which cybersecurity threat?
options: Phishing attacks
Ransomware — backups allow recovery without paying the ransom
Unauthorized access by former employees
Password spraying attacks
quizQuestions.explanation: Ransomware encrypts the victim's files and demands payment for the decryption key. Organizations with clean, recent, and tested backups can restore their systems without paying the ransom. The 3-2-1 backup rule (3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite) is best practice.

quizQuestions.question: When using a personal device for work purposes (BYOD), you must:
options: Use any device freely — personal devices are safer because they are not network-connected to practice systems
Enable device encryption, use only practice-approved apps, and follow the practice's BYOD policy
Only access non-patient information on personal devices
Register the device with your personal email rather than your work email
quizQuestions.explanation: BYOD policies typically require: device encryption, strong passcodes, use of approved secure apps, the ability for the practice to remotely wipe work data if the device is lost, and restrictions on storing ePHI on the device.

quizQuestions.question: The HIPAA Security Rule requires covered entities to perform a security risk analysis:
options: Only when implementing a new EHR system
At least annually and whenever significant operational changes occur
Every three years as part of the HIPAA audit cycle
Only after experiencing a breach
quizQuestions.explanation: HIPAA requires covered entities and business associates to conduct an accurate and thorough assessment of potential risks and vulnerabilities to ePHI — at least annually and whenever significant changes to the environment occur (new systems, mergers, new workflows).

title: Medical Records: Access, Release & Retention
description: Understand HIPAA's rules for releasing patient records, valid authorization requirements, retention timelines, and proper destruction of records.
lessonContent:
Key Terms
HIPAA = Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
PHI = Protected Health Information
OCR = Office for Civil Rights
TPO = Treatment, Payment, and Healthcare Operations

Before we start

Records requests are one of the most operationally common HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) touch points in a medical office — and one of the highest-risk areas for violations. In 2023, OCR's Right of Access Initiative passed 50 enforcement actions, with settlements ranging from $3,500 to $240,000. Most of those cases share a common thread: the practice received a request and either didn't respond, responded too slowly, or charged fees that weren't permissible. Understanding the rules around records isn't just compliance — it's operational competence for anyone who handles requests.

The designated record set — what patients can access

The Right of Access covers PHI in the "designated record set" — the records used to make decisions about the patient's care. In a medical office, that includes:

  • Medical records and billing records
  • Any other records used to make treatment, payment, or operations decisions

It does NOT include: psychotherapy notes (these have special protection and their own rules), information compiled in anticipation of litigation, and certain research-related information.

When a patient asks for "their records," they're almost certainly asking for the designated record set. If there's any ambiguity about what they're requesting, clarify — but don't use clarification as a delay tactic.

The 30-day clock — non-negotiable

Once you receive a records request, you have 30 days to fulfill it. A single 30-day extension is available if you notify the patient in writing before the initial 30 days expire and explain why more time is needed.

The clock starts when the request is received — not when you've verified identity, not when you've located the records, not when you've determined the fee. If you need additional information from the patient to process the request, reach out promptly — but the clock is still running.

What OCR has consistently found in enforcement actions: practices that simply didn't respond, practices that responded with "we're working on it" and then stopped, and practices that imposed unnecessary barriers (excessive fees, unreasonable format restrictions) that effectively blocked access.

Fees — what's allowed and what isn't

You can charge a reasonable, cost-based fee for:

  • Labor for copying — including the cost of staff time to locate and compile records
  • Supplies for creating paper copies or electronic media
  • Postage, if the patient requests mail delivery

You cannot charge:

  • Per-page fees that exceed the actual cost of the labor involved
  • Fees for searching for or retrieving records
  • Any fee that acts as a practical barrier to the patient receiving their records
  • A fee to a patient requesting electronic records when you maintain them electronically (you can charge for the media — a USB drive — but not for the labor)

Authorization requirements — the elements that make it valid

For disclosures outside of TPO, you need a valid written authorization. A HIPAA-compliant authorization must include all of the following:

  • A specific and meaningful description of the PHI to be used or disclosed
  • The identity of the person or class of persons authorized to make the disclosure
  • The identity of the person or class of persons to whom the disclosure may be made
  • The purpose of the use or disclosure
  • An expiration date or expiration event
  • The patient's signature and date

A defective authorization — one missing any required element — is not a valid authorization. Disclosing PHI based on a defective authorization is an impermissible disclosure.

The outstanding balance question: Can you withhold records because a patient hasn't paid their bill? No. An outstanding balance is not a permissible reason to deny access to records under HIPAA. This is one of the most frequently misunderstood rules — and one of the most frequently cited in OCR enforcement actions.

Retention requirements — federal floor, state ceiling

HIPAA requires covered entities to retain HIPAA-related policies, procedures, and documentation for 6 years from creation or last effective date. This is the federal minimum.

For actual medical records, most states set their own retention requirements — often longer than HIPAA's 6-year floor. The rule: follow whichever is longer, federal or state. In many states that means 7, 10, or more years for adult patient records.

When records are destroyed: they must be destroyed in a way that PHI cannot be reconstructed or recovered. Paper records require shredding. Electronic records require proper data destruction — not just deletion from the file system.

Key takeaways

  • Records requests must be fulfilled within 30 days. A single 30-day extension requires written notice to the patient before the first 30 days expire.
  • You can charge a cost-based fee. You cannot charge fees that act as barriers, and you cannot withhold records because of an outstanding balance.
  • Valid authorizations require six specific elements. A defective authorization doesn't protect you.
  • Retention: follow whichever is longer — federal 6-year minimum or your state's requirement. Destruction must render PHI unrecoverable.
  • Psychotherapy notes have separate, stricter rules and are not part of the standard designated record set.

Ready for the quiz. Six questions. You need 80% to pass.

quizQuestions.question: Under HIPAA's Right of Access, a covered entity may deny access to PHI if:
options: The record is longer than 100 pages
The patient owes an outstanding balance
Access is reasonably likely to endanger the life or safety of the patient or another person
The record is stored in a legacy system that is difficult to access
quizQuestions.explanation: HIPAA permits denial of access in limited circumstances, including when access is reasonably likely to endanger the life or physical safety of the patient or another person (e.g., mental health records in certain clinical situations). An outstanding balance is NOT a valid reason to deny access.

quizQuestions.question: A valid HIPAA authorization for release of PHI must include:
options: The patient's insurance card number and claim number
A description of the PHI to be disclosed, the purpose, the recipient, an expiration date, and the patient's signature
The treating provider's signature and medical license number
Approval from the practice's compliance officer
quizQuestions.explanation: A valid HIPAA authorization must contain: a description of the PHI to be used/disclosed, the identity of who may disclose it, the recipient, the purpose, an expiration date or event, the patient's signature, and the date. Missing any required element makes the authorization defective.

quizQuestions.question: Federal HIPAA regulations set a minimum medical records retention period of:
options: 3 years from date of service
6 years from the date the record was created, or when it was last in effect
10 years from the date of last service
HIPAA does not set a medical records retention period — only state law applies
quizQuestions.explanation: HIPAA requires covered entities to retain HIPAA-related policies, procedures, and documentation (including records of PHI disclosures) for 6 years from creation or last effective date. Note: State laws often set longer retention periods for actual medical records — always follow the longer of federal or state requirements.

quizQuestions.question: When releasing records in response to a court-ordered subpoena, the covered entity should:
options: Immediately comply without patient notification because it is a court order
Release only what is specified in the subpoena after verifying its validity and providing the patient an opportunity to seek a protective order if required
Deny the request since court subpoenas are not HIPAA-compliant
Contact OCR before releasing any records in response to a subpoena
quizQuestions.explanation: Subpoenas from attorneys (not judges) require the practice to either obtain patient authorization OR receive 'satisfactory assurance' that the requesting party has notified the patient or sought a protective order. Court orders signed by a judge may be complied with directly.

quizQuestions.question: Psychotherapy notes under HIPAA receive special protection. This means:
options: They are stored separately from the general medical record and require specific patient authorization to disclose, even for TPO
They may never be disclosed under any circumstances
They are protected only if the patient requests special protection in writing
They follow the same rules as all other PHI
quizQuestions.explanation: HIPAA gives psychotherapy notes the highest level of protection. They must be stored separately from the rest of the medical record and require a specific, separate authorization to disclose — even for treatment, payment, and healthcare operations (TPO) in most cases.

quizQuestions.question: A patient revokes a prior authorization for release of their PHI. Your practice must:
options: Honor the revocation for all future disclosures under that authorization, unless an action was already taken in reliance on it
Inform the recipient of the original authorization about the revocation
Obtain new authorization from a physician before honoring the revocation
Continue releasing the PHI since the original authorization was valid when signed
quizQuestions.explanation: A patient may revoke an authorization at any time in writing. The practice must honor the revocation going forward but is not required to reverse actions already taken in reliance on the valid authorization before it was revoked.

title: Business Associate Agreements Explained
description: Learn when a BAA is required, what it must contain, how to manage vendor BAA compliance, and consequences of operating without one.
lessonContent:
Key Terms
HIPAA = Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
PHI = Protected Health Information
ePHI = Electronic Protected Health Information
BAA = Business Associate Agreement
OCR = Office for Civil Rights

Before we start — vendor relationships are a major breach vector

In 2023, the Change Healthcare cyberattack — targeting a business associate of hundreds of health systems and practices — exposed the PHI of an estimated 190 million individuals, making it the largest healthcare data breach in U.S. history. The attack came through a vendor. The vendor was a business associate. This is why BAA management matters: your HIPAA compliance is only as strong as your weakest vendor relationship, and the legal and financial exposure from a vendor breach flows directly back to the covered entity.

What is a Business Associate

A Business Associate is any person or entity that, on behalf of a covered entity, performs functions or activities that involve the use or disclosure of PHI. The relationship doesn't have to be contractual — what matters is the function.

Common business associates in a medical practice:

  • EHR and practice management software vendors
  • Medical billing companies and clearinghouses
  • IT managed service providers with access to systems containing ePHI
  • Cloud storage providers where patient data is stored
  • Medical transcription services
  • Answering services that receive patient information
  • Shredding companies (access to paper PHI)
  • Attorneys or consultants who review records in the course of their work

What's generally NOT a business associate: vendors who provide services with no PHI access (the office supply company, the landscaper, the plumber).

What a BAA must contain

A Business Associate Agreement is a written contract that establishes the business associate's obligations regarding PHI. HIPAA specifies required elements:

  • Permitted uses and disclosures — the BA can only use PHI for the purposes specified in the agreement
  • A prohibition on further disclosure beyond what's permitted
  • Required security safeguards for ePHI
  • Breach notification obligations — the BA must report breaches to you without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days after discovery
  • A requirement to return or destroy all PHI upon termination of the agreement
  • Subcontractor flow-down — if the BA uses subcontractors who will access PHI, those subcontractors must also be bound by equivalent obligations

A BAA that's missing required elements isn't compliant. Many vendors provide their own BAA templates — review them against the required elements before signing.

When a BA breaches the BAA

If a business associate violates the terms of the BAA or violates HIPAA, the covered entity's responsibilities are:

  • Take reasonable steps to cure the breach or end the violation
  • If cure is not possible, terminate the contract
  • If termination is not feasible, report the problem to OCR

A covered entity that becomes aware of a BA's material violation and does nothing is exposed to liability. "We didn't know" is a defensible position. "We knew and took no action" is not.

The subcontractor chain: Your EHR vendor uses a cloud hosting provider to host your data. That cloud provider is a subcontractor of your business associate. Under the BAA framework, your EHR vendor is responsible for ensuring their subcontractors are also bound by HIPAA-equivalent obligations. Ask your EHR vendor whether they have BAAs with their subcontractors — it's a reasonable due diligence question.

Managing BAAs — practical obligations

A BAA that's signed and then forgotten isn't providing meaningful compliance value. Effective BAA management involves:

  • Maintaining an inventory of all vendors who have access to PHI and whether a BAA is in place
  • Reviewing BAA terms periodically — especially when the scope of a vendor relationship changes
  • Tracking BAA expiration dates if your BAAs have them (many are perpetual, but some have terms)
  • Obtaining new or updated BAAs when HIPAA regulations change significantly
  • Ensuring the BAA addresses the actual services the vendor provides — not just a generic agreement

Your practice's vendor management system should track BAA status for every vendor with PHI access. If you're responsible for onboarding new vendors, verify BAA status before that vendor is given access to any PHI.

What happens when a BA has a breach

When your business associate experiences a breach involving your patients' PHI, the BA must notify you without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days after they discover it. The BA provides you with the information you need to fulfill your own breach notification obligations — affected individuals, nature of the PHI, date of breach, etc.

The covered entity — your practice — is ultimately responsible for notifying affected patients and OCR, even though the breach happened at the BA. The 60-day clock for patient notification runs from the BA's discovery of the breach (when they should have notified you), not from when they actually told you.

Key takeaways

  • A Business Associate is any entity that performs functions involving PHI on your behalf. Billing companies, EHR vendors, IT providers, transcription services — all require BAAs.
  • BAAs must contain specific required elements. A generic confidentiality clause is not a BAA.
  • When a BA has a breach, they notify you; you notify patients and OCR. The clock runs from their discovery.
  • If a BA violates HIPAA, you must act — cure the problem, terminate if necessary, or report to OCR. Inaction is not a defensible response.
  • Maintain an inventory of all vendors with PHI access and their BAA status. Verify before granting any new vendor PHI access.

Ready for the quiz. Six questions. You need 80% to pass.

quizQuestions.question: Under HIPAA, a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) is required when:
options: Any vendor sends an invoice to the practice
A vendor or contractor will create, receive, maintain, or transmit PHI on behalf of the covered entity
A covered entity purchases software from a technology company
An employee is promoted to a management role with access to billing records
quizQuestions.explanation: A BAA is required before engaging any person or entity (business associate) that will perform functions or activities on behalf of the covered entity that involve creating, receiving, maintaining, or transmitting PHI.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following vendors would generally require a BAA?
options: The practice's janitorial service (no PHI access)
An EHR software vendor that hosts patient data
A plumbing company making repairs to the office
A landscaping company maintaining the practice's grounds
quizQuestions.explanation: An EHR vendor that hosts or accesses patient data is clearly a business associate and requires a BAA. Vendors who provide services without accessing PHI (janitorial, plumbing, landscaping) are generally not business associates.

quizQuestions.question: A business associate experiences a security incident involving PHI. Under HIPAA, they must notify the covered entity:
options: Within 24 hours of discovery
Within 60 days of discovery
Without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days after discovery
Only if the breach affects 500 or more individuals
quizQuestions.explanation: Business associates must notify the covered entity of a breach 'without unreasonable delay and in no case later than 60 days after discovery.' The covered entity then determines whether it constitutes a reportable breach and manages notifications to individuals and OCR.

quizQuestions.question: If a Business Associate violates the terms of the BAA, the covered entity's responsibility is to:
options: Immediately report the BA to OCR
Take reasonable steps to cure the breach or end the violation; terminate the contract if cure is not possible
Pay the penalty on the BA's behalf since the CE contracted with them
File a lawsuit against the BA before taking any other action
quizQuestions.explanation: Under HIPAA, when a covered entity learns of a material breach by a business associate, it must: (1) take reasonable steps to cure the breach or end the violation, and (2) if cure is not possible, terminate the contract and report the problem to OCR.

quizQuestions.question: A BAA must include a provision requiring the business associate to:
options: Guarantee zero breaches of PHI over the contract period
Report breaches of unsecured PHI to the covered entity
Submit to annual HIPAA audits by the covered entity
Maintain a full-time Privacy Officer on staff
quizQuestions.explanation: A HIPAA-compliant BAA must include terms requiring the business associate to: use PHI only as permitted, report breaches of unsecured PHI, return or destroy PHI at termination, and ensure subcontractors agree to the same obligations.

quizQuestions.question: When a Business Associate relationship ends, the business associate must:
options: Archive all PHI for 10 years before deleting it
Return or destroy all PHI received from or created on behalf of the covered entity
Transfer all PHI to the covered entity's new vendor immediately
Retain PHI for 6 years as required by HIPAA documentation retention rules
quizQuestions.explanation: Upon termination of the BAA, the business associate must return or destroy all PHI — in all forms — that it maintains in any form. If return or destruction is not feasible, the BAA must include provisions to extend its protections for as long as the BA retains the PHI.

title: Infection Control & Prevention in Medical Practices
description: Learn standard precautions, hand hygiene protocols, surface disinfection, and infection prevention strategies specific to outpatient medical settings.
lessonContent:
Key Terms
PPE = Personal Protective Equipment
CDC = Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
OSHA = Occupational Safety and Health Administration
OPIM = Other Potentially Infectious Materials

Before we start — why this actually matters

A family medicine practice in Georgia experienced an outbreak of Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) among patients after staff failed to properly disinfect exam tables between visits. Six patients developed skin infections, two required hospitalization, and the practice faced lawsuits and a state health department investigation. The root cause was a breakdown in basic infection control — not a lack of sophisticated equipment, but a failure to follow fundamental precautions consistently.

In outpatient medical settings, infection prevention is every staff member's responsibility. Understanding Standard Precautions, hand hygiene, and proper use of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) is essential to protecting both patients and healthcare workers.

Standard Precautions — the foundation

Standard Precautions are a set of infection prevention practices that apply to ALL patients, regardless of suspected or confirmed infection status, in any healthcare setting. They are the minimum level of infection control that should be used in the care of every patient, every time. Standard Precautions are based on the principle that all blood, body fluids (except sweat), non-intact skin, and mucous membranes may contain transmissible infectious agents.

Standard Precautions include:

  • Hand hygiene
  • Use of PPE (gloves, gowns, masks, eye protection) based on anticipated exposure
  • Respiratory hygiene and cough etiquette
  • Safe injection practices
  • Safe handling of potentially contaminated equipment and surfaces
  • Proper handling and disposal of sharps and regulated waste

Hand hygiene — the single most effective measure

According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), proper hand hygiene is the single most effective way to prevent the spread of infections in healthcare settings. Hand hygiene should be performed:

  • Before and after every direct patient contact
  • After touching contaminated surfaces or equipment
  • Before putting on gloves and after removing gloves
  • After contact with blood, body fluids, or other potentially infectious materials (OPIM)
  • Before performing aseptic procedures
  • After using the restroom

Alcohol-based hand rub (ABHR) is the preferred method when hands are not visibly soiled. Soap and water should be used when hands are visibly dirty, after caring for patients with C. difficile or norovirus, and after using the restroom.

Common mistake: Wearing gloves does NOT replace hand hygiene. You must perform hand hygiene before putting on gloves and immediately after removing them. Gloves can develop micro-tears during use, and bacteria can multiply rapidly on gloved hands.

Transmission-Based Precautions

Transmission-Based Precautions are used in addition to Standard Precautions when Standard Precautions alone may not be sufficient to prevent transmission of certain infectious agents. There are three categories:

  • Contact Precautions: For infections spread by direct or indirect contact (e.g., MRSA, VRE, C. difficile). Requires gown and gloves for all patient contact.
  • Droplet Precautions: For infections spread through respiratory droplets (e.g., influenza, pertussis, mumps). Requires a surgical mask within 6 feet of the patient.
  • Airborne Precautions: For infections spread through airborne particles (e.g., tuberculosis, measles, chickenpox). Requires an N95 respirator or higher, and the patient should be placed in an airborne infection isolation room (negative pressure).

Proper PPE donning and doffing sequence

The correct sequence for donning (putting on) PPE is:

  1. Gown — put on first, secure at the neck and waist
  2. Mask or Respirator — secure ties or elastic bands, fit to face
  3. Eye Protection — goggles or face shield, placed over face and adjusted
  4. Gloves — put on last, extend gloves over the cuffs of the gown

The correct sequence for doffing (removing) PPE is the reverse of contamination risk — remove the most contaminated items first:

  1. Gloves (most contaminated)
  2. Gown
  3. Eye protection
  4. Mask or respirator (touched last, as it protects your respiratory system)

Perform hand hygiene immediately after removing all PPE.

Real scenario: A medical assistant enters an exam room to take vitals on a patient with suspected influenza. She puts on gloves first, then a gown, then a mask. This incorrect donning order means the gloves were contaminated when she adjusted her gown and mask. The correct order — gown, mask, then gloves — keeps the gloves clean until the final step.

Environmental cleaning and disinfection

Proper surface disinfection is critical in outpatient settings where high-touch surfaces (exam tables, door handles, countertops, keyboards) can harbor pathogens. The Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) and the CDC recommend:

  • Clean and disinfect exam rooms and high-touch surfaces between every patient
  • Use EPA-registered hospital-grade disinfectants with appropriate contact time
  • Follow the manufacturer's instructions for dilution and dwell time
  • Clean visibly soiled surfaces before applying disinfectant

Key takeaways

  • Standard Precautions apply to ALL patients regardless of infection status.
  • Hand hygiene is the single most effective way to prevent healthcare-associated infections.
  • Transmission-Based Precautions (Contact, Droplet, Airborne) are used in addition to Standard Precautions for specific pathogens.
  • The correct PPE donning order is: Gown, Mask/Respirator, Eye Protection, Gloves.
  • Clean and disinfect between every patient — use EPA-registered disinfectants with proper contact time.

You're ready for the quiz. Five questions on infection control and prevention. You need 80% to pass and earn your certificate.

quizQuestions.question: What are Standard Precautions?
options: Precautions taken only when a patient is known to be infectious
A set of infection prevention practices applied to ALL patients regardless of suspected or confirmed infection status
Measures required only during surgical procedures
Protocols used exclusively in hospital intensive care units
quizQuestions.explanation: Standard Precautions are the minimum infection prevention practices that apply to all patient care, regardless of suspected or confirmed infection status, in any healthcare setting.

quizQuestions.question: According to the CDC, the single most effective way to prevent the spread of infections in healthcare settings is:
options: Wearing gloves at all times
Using antibacterial cleaning products on all surfaces
Proper hand hygiene
Wearing an N95 respirator
quizQuestions.explanation: The CDC identifies hand hygiene as the single most important practice for reducing the transmission of infectious agents in healthcare settings.

quizQuestions.question: When should hand hygiene be performed in a clinical setting?
options: Only before eating lunch
Before and after every patient contact, after touching contaminated surfaces, before putting on and after removing gloves
Only after visibly soiling your hands
Once per hour during patient care
quizQuestions.explanation: Hand hygiene should be performed before and after direct patient contact, after contact with potentially contaminated surfaces, before and after using gloves, and after contact with body fluids.

quizQuestions.question: Transmission-Based Precautions are used in ADDITION to Standard Precautions when:
options: A patient requests extra protective measures
Standard Precautions alone may not be sufficient to prevent transmission of certain infectious agents
The practice runs out of standard cleaning supplies
Only during winter flu season
quizQuestions.explanation: Transmission-Based Precautions (Contact, Droplet, and Airborne) are used in addition to Standard Precautions for patients known or suspected to be infected with agents that require additional control measures.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the correct order for donning (putting on) PPE?
options: Gloves → Gown → Mask → Eye Protection
Gown → Mask/Respirator → Eye Protection → Gloves
Mask → Gloves → Gown → Eye Protection
Eye Protection → Gloves → Gown → Mask
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct donning sequence is: (1) Gown, (2) Mask or Respirator, (3) Eye Protection, (4) Gloves. This order ensures each layer is properly secured before the next is applied.

title: Emergency Preparedness for Healthcare Facilities
description: Covers emergency planning, natural disaster response, active threat protocols, and continuity of operations planning for medical practices.
lessonContent:
Key Terms
CMS = Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services
COOP = Continuity of Operations Plan
EOP = Emergency Operations Plan

Before we start — why this actually matters

When Hurricane Harvey hit Houston in 2017, dozens of medical practices were forced to close with no plan for patient continuity. Practices that had emergency preparedness plans — including off-site data backups, communication trees, and pre-established agreements with other facilities — were able to resume operations within days. Those without plans lost weeks, and in some cases, permanently lost patient records and revenue.

The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) requires all Medicare- and Medicaid-participating healthcare providers to meet specific emergency preparedness requirements. Even if your practice doesn't participate in these programs, having a robust emergency plan protects your patients, staff, and business.

CMS Emergency Preparedness requirements

CMS requires healthcare facilities that participate in Medicare and Medicaid to comply with Emergency Preparedness requirements. These requirements were finalized in 2016 and apply to 17 types of providers and suppliers. The four core elements are:

  1. Risk Assessment and Emergency Plan: Conduct a risk assessment using an all-hazards approach, and develop an emergency plan based on the assessment.
  2. Communication Plan: Establish a plan for communicating with staff, patients, emergency services, and other healthcare providers during an emergency.
  3. Policies and Procedures: Develop and implement policies for emergency operations, including provisions for patient care, staffing, and supply management.
  4. Training and Testing: Conduct training on emergency procedures and test the plan through drills and exercises.
Marketing is not an emergency function: Emergency preparedness plans focus exclusively on life safety, patient care continuity, and operational recovery. Plans to increase marketing during emergencies, manage PR, or promote services are not part of the CMS emergency preparedness framework.

Training and testing requirements

CMS requires healthcare facilities to conduct emergency preparedness training and testing at least annually. Specifically:

  • All new and existing staff must be trained on the emergency plan and their roles during emergencies.
  • The facility must participate in a full-scale, community-based exercise at least every other year. In the alternating year, the facility may conduct a facility-based drill or tabletop exercise.
  • Training must be documented, and the plan must be reviewed and updated at least annually based on lessons learned from drills and actual events.

Emergency priorities — patient safety first

During any emergency at a medical practice — whether a power outage, fire, severe weather, or active threat — the first priority is always patient safety. Staff should:

  • Ensure patient safety and stabilize any patients currently receiving care
  • Activate the emergency operations plan
  • Secure medications, especially controlled substances
  • Account for all staff and patients in the facility
  • Communicate status to emergency services and staff members not in the building
Real scenario: A power outage hits your practice during afternoon clinic hours. Three patients are mid-procedure in different exam rooms. The correct response: stabilize all patients immediately, activate battery-powered emergency lighting, secure medications, and implement the communication plan. Calling the electric company is important but secondary to patient safety.

Continuity of Operations Planning (COOP)

A Continuity of Operations Plan (COOP) addresses how the practice will continue its essential functions during and after an emergency. A COOP typically includes:

  • Identification of essential functions that must continue (patient care, medication management, records access)
  • Alternate operating locations if the primary site is unusable
  • Delegation of authority — who makes decisions if the practice owner/administrator is unreachable
  • Vital records protection — how patient records, financial data, and operational documents are backed up and accessible remotely
  • Recovery priorities — the order in which functions will be restored as conditions allow

Key takeaways

  • CMS requires emergency preparedness plans from all Medicare/Medicaid-participating providers.
  • Plans must include risk assessment, communication plan, policies/procedures, and training/testing.
  • Training and drills must be conducted at least annually; community-wide exercises every other year.
  • Patient safety is always the first priority in any emergency.
  • A COOP ensures essential functions continue during and after an emergency.

You're ready for the quiz. Five questions on emergency preparedness. You need 80% to pass and earn your certificate.

quizQuestions.question: Which federal agency requires healthcare facilities to have an Emergency Preparedness plan?
options: The FDA
The Joint Commission and CMS (Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services)
The IRS
The Department of Education
quizQuestions.explanation: CMS requires all Medicare- and Medicaid-participating providers to comply with Emergency Preparedness requirements, including risk assessments, emergency plans, and communication plans.

quizQuestions.question: An Emergency Preparedness plan for a medical practice should include all of the following EXCEPT:
options: A communication plan for staff, patients, and emergency services
Policies and procedures for emergency operations
A plan to increase marketing during emergencies
Training and testing (drills) of the emergency plan
quizQuestions.explanation: Emergency Preparedness plans must include: risk assessment, emergency plan, communication plan, policies and procedures for operations during emergencies, and training/testing. Marketing plans are not a component.

quizQuestions.question: How often should a medical practice conduct an emergency preparedness drill or exercise?
options: Once every 5 years
Only after an actual emergency occurs
At least annually, with CMS requiring participation in a community-wide exercise every other year
Only when a new employee is hired
quizQuestions.explanation: CMS requires healthcare facilities to conduct training and testing of emergency plans at least annually. Facilities must participate in a full-scale community-based exercise at least every two years.

quizQuestions.question: During a power outage at a medical practice, what is the FIRST priority?
options: Resume scheduling appointments
Ensure patient safety and stabilize any patients currently receiving care
Call the electric company
Post a notice on the practice website
quizQuestions.explanation: The first priority in any emergency is patient safety. Staff should stabilize patients currently receiving care, secure medications, and activate the emergency operations plan.

quizQuestions.question: A continuity of operations plan (COOP) addresses:
options: How the practice will continue essential functions during and after an emergency
How to handle patient billing disputes
Annual employee performance reviews
New patient onboarding procedures
quizQuestions.explanation: A COOP outlines how an organization will continue essential functions during and after an emergency, including alternate sites, delegation of authority, vital records protection, and recovery priorities.

title: Managing PHI in Cloud & Mobile Environments
description: Covers HIPAA requirements for cloud-hosted EHR systems, mobile devices, texting PHI, telehealth platforms, and securing ePHI in modern technology environments.
lessonContent:
Key Terms
HIPAA = Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
PHI = Protected Health Information
ePHI = Electronic Protected Health Information
BAA = Business Associate Agreement
EHR = Electronic Health Record

Before we start — why this actually matters

A pediatric practice in Oregon stored patient records in a popular cloud-based Electronic Health Record (EHR) system but never signed a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the cloud provider. When the provider experienced a data breach affecting 12,000 patient records, the practice was held jointly responsible. The Office for Civil Rights (OCR) imposed a $310,000 fine — not for the breach itself, but for failing to have a BAA in place with their cloud vendor.

Modern healthcare runs on cloud services, mobile devices, and telehealth platforms. The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) doesn't prohibit using these technologies — but it does require specific safeguards. This course covers how to use cloud and mobile technologies while keeping electronic Protected Health Information (ePHI) secure.

Cloud services and HIPAA — the BAA requirement

When a medical practice uses a cloud-based EHR system or any cloud service that stores, processes, or transmits ePHI, the cloud provider is considered a business associate under HIPAA. As a business associate, the cloud provider must sign a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) before receiving any ePHI.

A BAA is not just a formality — it establishes the cloud provider's legal obligations to protect ePHI, including:

  • Implementing appropriate administrative, physical, and technical safeguards
  • Reporting security incidents and breaches to the covered entity
  • Ensuring any subcontractors that handle ePHI also sign BAAs
  • Returning or destroying ePHI at the termination of the agreement
No BAA = HIPAA violation: Using a cloud service to store or process ePHI without a signed BAA is a HIPAA violation — regardless of whether a breach occurs. Many popular consumer cloud services (Google Drive personal accounts, Dropbox free tier, iCloud) do not offer BAAs by default.

Evaluating cloud services before adoption

Before using a new cloud-based application to store or process patient data, a practice should:

  1. Conduct a security assessment of the vendor's security controls, certifications (SOC 2, HITRUST), and track record
  2. Sign a BAA with the vendor before any ePHI is transmitted
  3. Verify the service meets HIPAA requirements — encryption at rest and in transit, access controls, audit logging, and breach notification capabilities
  4. Include the service in your risk assessment — document the risks and mitigations associated with the cloud service

Texting and messaging — what's compliant?

Standard consumer text messaging (SMS, iMessage) is not compliant with HIPAA for transmitting PHI. Standard SMS is not encrypted end-to-end and does not meet HIPAA's transmission security requirements. Messages can be intercepted, stored on carrier servers, and are often backed up to unsecured cloud accounts.

Practices should use HIPAA-compliant secure messaging platforms that provide:

  • End-to-end encryption
  • Access controls and user authentication
  • Audit logging of all messages
  • Remote wipe capabilities
  • Message expiration/auto-delete features

Mobile device security requirements

Any mobile device that accesses ePHI — whether practice-owned or personal (BYOD) — must have the following safeguards:

  • Encryption at rest and in transit
  • Strong authentication — passwords, PINs, or biometric locks
  • Remote wipe capability — the ability to erase all data if the device is lost or stolen
  • Automatic screen lock after a short period of inactivity
  • Regular security updates — keep the operating system and apps up to date
Real scenario: A medical assistant uses her personal smartphone with the practice's EHR app installed. The phone is left in a restaurant. The correct response: immediately report to the Security Officer, initiate a remote wipe of the device, document the incident, and conduct a breach risk assessment using the four-factor test (nature and extent of PHI involved, unauthorized person who accessed it, whether PHI was actually viewed, and mitigations applied).

Lost or stolen devices — incident response

When a device with access to ePHI is lost or stolen, the practice must take immediate action:

  1. Report immediately to the Security Officer or designated privacy contact
  2. Initiate remote wipe to erase the device
  3. Document the incident with details of what data was on the device
  4. Conduct a breach risk assessment — use the four-factor analysis to determine whether notification is required
  5. Implement corrective action to prevent recurrence

Key takeaways

  • Cloud providers that handle ePHI are business associates and must sign a BAA.
  • Always conduct a security assessment, sign a BAA, and verify HIPAA compliance before using a new cloud service.
  • Standard SMS/texting is not HIPAA-compliant for transmitting PHI — use encrypted secure messaging platforms.
  • Mobile devices accessing ePHI must have encryption, strong authentication, remote wipe, and auto-lock.
  • Lost/stolen devices require immediate reporting, remote wipe, documentation, and breach risk assessment.

You're ready for the quiz. Five questions on managing PHI in cloud and mobile environments. You need 80% to pass and earn your certificate.

quizQuestions.question: When a medical practice uses a cloud-based EHR system, the cloud provider is considered a:
options: Patient
Covered entity
Business associate that must sign a BAA
Government regulator
quizQuestions.explanation: Any cloud service provider that creates, receives, maintains, or transmits ePHI on behalf of a covered entity is a business associate under HIPAA and must sign a BAA.

quizQuestions.question: Is it permissible under HIPAA to text patient PHI using a standard consumer text messaging app (e.g., iMessage, SMS)?
options: Yes, as long as you delete the text afterward
Yes, texting is always secure
No, standard SMS/texting is not encrypted end-to-end and does not meet HIPAA requirements for transmission security
Only if the patient initiates the text
quizQuestions.explanation: Standard SMS is not encrypted and does not meet HIPAA's transmission security requirements. Practices should use HIPAA-compliant secure messaging platforms that offer encryption, access controls, and audit logging.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a REQUIRED safeguard for mobile devices that access ePHI?
options: The device must be manufactured in the United States
Encryption, strong passwords/biometrics, remote wipe capability, and automatic screen lock
The device must cost more than $500
The device must be owned by the practice (personal devices can never be used)
quizQuestions.explanation: Mobile devices accessing ePHI must have: encryption (at rest and in transit), strong authentication (password/biometric), remote wipe capability, automatic screen lock, and regular security updates.

quizQuestions.question: Before using a new cloud-based application to store or process patient data, a practice should:
options: Start using it immediately since cloud services are always secure
