Conduct a security assessment, sign a BAA with the vendor, and verify the service meets HIPAA requirements
Ask patients for their consent to use the cloud service
Only use free versions of cloud services
quizQuestions.explanation: Before adopting any cloud service for PHI, the practice must evaluate its security controls, sign a BAA, verify HIPAA compliance, and include it in the practice's risk assessment.

quizQuestions.question: A staff member loses their personal smartphone which has the practice's EHR app installed. What should happen?
options: Nothing — it's their personal phone
Buy them a new phone
Immediately report to the Security Officer, initiate remote wipe, document the incident, and conduct a breach risk assessment
Wait to see if any patient data is compromised before taking action
quizQuestions.explanation: Any loss of a device with access to ePHI must be reported immediately. The Security Officer should initiate remote wipe, document the incident, assess whether a breach occurred using the 4-factor test, and take corrective action.

title: Medical Waste Management & Disposal
description: Learn proper segregation, handling, storage, and disposal of regulated medical waste including sharps, biohazardous materials, pharmaceutical waste, and chemical waste.
lessonContent:
Key Terms
OSHA = Occupational Safety and Health Administration
OPIM = Other Potentially Infectious Materials
PPE = Personal Protective Equipment
DEA = Drug Enforcement Administration
EPA = Environmental Protection Agency

Before we start — why this actually matters

A dermatology practice in New Jersey was fined $45,000 after a custodial worker was stuck by an improperly disposed needle found in a regular trash bag. The practice had sharps containers in exam rooms, but staff had been disposing of used sharps on the counter instead of directly into the container. The needle ended up in the regular waste stream. One lapse in waste segregation resulted in a serious injury and a five-figure fine from the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA).

Proper medical waste management protects staff, patients, the public, and the environment. The rules are straightforward — but they must be followed consistently, starting at the point where waste is generated.

Waste segregation at the point of generation

Regulated medical waste must be separated from regular trash at the point of generation — meaning the exact location where the waste is created. This is the single most important principle in medical waste management. If waste is mixed at the point of generation, the entire waste stream becomes contaminated and must be treated as regulated medical waste.

The four main categories of medical waste are:

  • Sharps waste: Needles, scalpels, lancets, broken glass, and any item that can puncture or cut
  • Biohazardous (infectious) waste: Blood-soaked bandages, pathological waste, and items saturated with blood or other potentially infectious materials (OPIM)
  • Pharmaceutical waste: Expired, unused, or contaminated medications
  • Chemical waste: Disinfectants, solvents, and other hazardous chemicals

Sharps disposal

Used needles and other sharps must be disposed of in FDA-cleared, puncture-resistant sharps containers that are:

  • Closeable — the container can be securely sealed when full
  • Leakproof — liquids cannot escape from the sides or bottom
  • Labeled — marked with the biohazard symbol or color-coded red
  • Puncture-resistant — sharps cannot penetrate the container walls

Sharps containers must be replaced when they reach the three-quarters (3/4) full line or the fill line marked on the container. Overfilling sharps containers is one of the leading causes of needlestick injuries in outpatient settings.

Never recap needles: OSHA prohibits recapping used needles by hand. Recapping is one of the most common causes of needlestick injuries. Dispose of the needle and syringe as a unit directly into the sharps container immediately after use.

Biohazardous (infectious) waste

Biohazardous waste includes items contaminated with blood or other potentially infectious materials (OPIM), pathological waste (tissues, organs), and cultures or stocks of infectious agents. Examples include:

  • Blood-soaked bandages and dressings
  • Surgical specimens and pathological waste
  • Items saturated or dripping with blood
  • Microbiological cultures and associated materials

Biohazardous waste must be placed in red bags or containers marked with the biohazard symbol. Items that are lightly spotted with blood (such as bandages with a small blood spot) are generally considered regular waste — the threshold is saturation or dripping.

Pharmaceutical waste

Expired or unused medications cannot simply be thrown in the trash or flushed. Pharmaceutical waste must be handled according to the practice's pharmaceutical waste management plan, following Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), state, and Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA) regulations:

  • Non-hazardous pharmaceutical waste: Disposed of through an authorized pharmaceutical waste collection program
  • Hazardous pharmaceutical waste: Managed under EPA Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA) rules
  • Controlled substances: Require additional DEA documentation, inventory tracking, and witnessed destruction. Two authorized personnel must witness and document the destruction of controlled substances.
Real scenario: Your practice has a cabinet of expired medications including some Schedule II controlled substances. You cannot simply dispose of them with regular pharmaceutical waste. The controlled substances must be inventoried, witnessed by two employees, destroyed through a DEA-authorized method, and documented with DEA Form 41 or through an authorized reverse distributor.

Key takeaways

  • Segregate regulated medical waste from regular trash at the point of generation.
  • Sharps go in FDA-cleared, puncture-resistant, closeable, leakproof, labeled containers.
  • Replace sharps containers when they reach the 3/4 full line.
  • Biohazardous waste includes blood-soaked items, pathological waste, and OPIM-contaminated materials.
  • Pharmaceutical waste — especially controlled substances — requires specific handling per EPA and DEA regulations.

You're ready for the quiz. Five questions on medical waste management. You need 80% to pass and earn your certificate.

quizQuestions.question: Regulated medical waste must be segregated from regular trash at the:
options: End of the business day
Point of generation — where the waste is created
Loading dock before pickup
Recycling center
quizQuestions.explanation: Regulated medical waste must be segregated from regular waste at the point of generation — the location where it is produced — to prevent contamination and ensure proper handling throughout the waste stream.

quizQuestions.question: Used needles and other sharps should be disposed of in:
options: Regular trash cans with lids
Red biohazard bags
FDA-cleared, puncture-resistant sharps containers that are closeable, leakproof, and labeled
Any sealed plastic container
quizQuestions.explanation: OSHA requires sharps to be disposed of in FDA-cleared, puncture-resistant containers that are closeable, leakproof, and properly labeled with the biohazard symbol or color-coded red.

quizQuestions.question: Sharps containers should be replaced when they are:
options: Completely full to the top
Three-quarters (¾) full
Only when they are damaged
Every 24 hours regardless of fill level
quizQuestions.explanation: Sharps containers should be replaced when they reach the three-quarters (¾) full line or the fill line marked on the container. Overfilling increases the risk of needlestick injuries.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is classified as biohazardous (infectious) waste?
options: Empty IV bags with no blood contamination
Paper towels used to dry clean hands
Blood-soaked bandages, pathological waste, and items saturated with blood or potentially infectious materials
Food waste from the break room
quizQuestions.explanation: Biohazardous waste includes items contaminated with blood or other potentially infectious materials (OPIM), pathological waste, and cultures/stocks of infectious agents.

quizQuestions.question: Pharmaceutical waste (expired or unused medications) should be:
options: Flushed down the sink
Placed in regular trash
Handled according to the practice's pharmaceutical waste management plan, with controlled substances tracked per DEA requirements
Given to patients who need them
quizQuestions.explanation: Pharmaceutical waste must be segregated, tracked, and disposed of according to EPA, state, and DEA regulations. Controlled substances require additional documentation and witnessed destruction.

title: Preventing Discrimination & Harassment in Healthcare
description: Covers Title VII, ADA, and state anti-discrimination laws as they apply to healthcare workplaces. Includes recognizing, reporting, and preventing harassment.
lessonContent:
Key Terms
ADA = Americans with Disabilities Act
EEOC = Equal Employment Opportunity Commission
HR = Human Resources

Before we start — why this actually matters

An orthopedic practice in Texas paid $425,000 to settle a sexual harassment lawsuit after a physician made repeated unwanted comments and advances toward a medical assistant over several months. The medical assistant had reported the behavior to the office manager, who told her to "just avoid him." The practice had no formal anti-harassment policy, no designated reporting channel, and no investigation was ever conducted. The settlement included back pay, emotional distress damages, attorney's fees, and mandatory training for all staff.

Workplace discrimination and harassment are not just ethical issues — they carry severe legal, financial, and reputational consequences. Federal and state laws provide robust protections for employees, and healthcare practices are not exempt.

Title VII of the Civil Rights Act

Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 prohibits employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex (including pregnancy, sexual orientation, and gender identity), and national origin. Title VII applies to employers with 15 or more employees, including private-sector healthcare practices.

Title VII protections cover all aspects of employment: hiring, firing, pay, promotions, job assignments, training, benefits, and any other term or condition of employment.

When harassment becomes unlawful

Workplace harassment becomes unlawful when it is severe or pervasive enough to create a work environment that a reasonable person would consider intimidating, hostile, or abusive. Harassment can also be unlawful when enduring the offensive conduct becomes a condition of continued employment (quid pro quo harassment).

Important distinctions:

  • A single minor incident (e.g., an isolated off-color joke) typically does not rise to unlawful harassment, though it should still be addressed.
  • A pattern of unwelcome conduct — even if each individual instance seems minor — can constitute a hostile work environment.
  • The harasser can be a supervisor, coworker, or even a non-employee (such as a patient or vendor).
  • Constructive criticism about job performance is NOT harassment.

Reporting obligations

If you witness or experience harassment in the workplace, you should report it through the practice's designated reporting channel — typically HR, the practice manager, or a designated compliance officer. Key points:

  • The practice is legally obligated to investigate complaints promptly and thoroughly
  • The practice must take appropriate corrective action if harassment is substantiated
  • Employees do not need to confront the harasser directly before filing a report
  • Reports should be as specific as possible: dates, locations, witnesses, and descriptions of the conduct
Management liability: If a supervisor or manager knows about harassment and fails to act, the practice can be held liable — even if the victim never filed a formal complaint. "I didn't know" is not a defense if the employer should have known.

The Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA)

The Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) prohibits discrimination against qualified individuals with disabilities in all aspects of employment. Under the ADA, an employer must provide reasonable accommodations that do not impose an undue hardship on the employer.

Reasonable accommodations may include:

  • Modified work schedules or break times
  • Ergonomic equipment or assistive technology
  • Job restructuring or reassignment to a vacant position
  • Accessible workspaces and facilities

The ADA does NOT require employers to eliminate essential job functions, lower production standards, or provide accommodations that create an undue financial or operational burden.

Retaliation is prohibited

Retaliation against an employee who files a discrimination or harassment complaint is prohibited by federal law — and retaliation itself constitutes a separate, independent violation. Retaliation includes any adverse action taken because an employee:

  • Filed a complaint of discrimination or harassment (internally or with the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC))
  • Participated in an investigation as a witness
  • Opposed conduct they reasonably believed to be unlawful

Retaliation can take many forms: termination, demotion, schedule changes, exclusion from meetings, negative performance reviews, or any action that would discourage a reasonable person from reporting.

Real scenario: A receptionist files a harassment complaint against her supervisor. Two weeks later, her schedule is changed from the day shift she has worked for three years to the night shift. Even if the supervisor claims the change is for "business reasons," this timing raises a strong inference of retaliation. The practice could face liability for both the original harassment and the retaliatory schedule change.

Key takeaways

  • Title VII protects employees from discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, and national origin.
  • Harassment is unlawful when it is severe or pervasive enough to create a hostile work environment.
  • Report harassment through the practice's designated channel — HR, practice manager, or compliance officer.
  • The ADA requires reasonable accommodations that do not impose undue hardship.
  • Retaliation for filing a complaint is a separate federal violation.

You're ready for the quiz. Five questions on preventing discrimination and harassment. You need 80% to pass and earn your certificate.

quizQuestions.question: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act protects employees from discrimination based on:
options: Age only
Race, color, religion, sex, and national origin
Political party affiliation
Social media posts
quizQuestions.explanation: Title VII prohibits employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex (including pregnancy, sexual orientation, and gender identity), and national origin.

quizQuestions.question: Workplace harassment becomes unlawful when:
options: A coworker tells a single off-color joke
It is severe or pervasive enough to create a work environment that a reasonable person would consider intimidating, hostile, or abusive
An employee is simply annoyed by a coworker
A supervisor gives constructive criticism about job performance
quizQuestions.explanation: Harassment becomes unlawful when it is severe or pervasive enough to create a work environment that a reasonable person would consider intimidating, hostile, or abusive, or when enduring it becomes a condition of continued employment.

quizQuestions.question: If you witness or experience harassment in the workplace, you should:
options: Ignore it and hope it stops
Confront the harasser yourself with physical force
Report it through the practice's designated reporting channel (e.g., HR, practice manager, compliance officer)
Post about it on social media
quizQuestions.explanation: Employees should report harassment through the practice's established reporting channels. The practice is required to investigate complaints promptly and take appropriate corrective action.

quizQuestions.question: An employer's obligation under the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) includes:
options: Guaranteeing employment to all disabled applicants
Providing reasonable accommodations that do not impose an undue hardship on the employer
Eliminating all job qualifications for disabled applicants
Lowering performance standards for employees with disabilities
quizQuestions.explanation: Under the ADA, employers must provide reasonable accommodations to qualified individuals with disabilities unless doing so would impose an undue hardship on the employer.

quizQuestions.question: Retaliation against an employee who files a discrimination or harassment complaint is:
options: Acceptable if the complaint was found to be without merit
Legal as long as the employer documents a business reason
Prohibited by federal law — retaliation itself is a separate violation
Only illegal if the retaliation involves termination
quizQuestions.explanation: Retaliation against employees who report discrimination or harassment, participate in investigations, or oppose unlawful practices is strictly prohibited under Title VII, the ADA, and other federal statutes.

title: Drug-Free Workplace Policy Training
description: Understand the Drug-Free Workplace Act requirements, employee responsibilities, consequences of violations, and available resources for substance abuse assistance.
lessonContent:
Key Terms
EAP = Employee Assistance Program
HR = Human Resources

Before we start — why this actually matters

A surgical center in Florida lost its Medicare participation status after a nurse was found to have been diverting controlled substances for personal use for over a year. The investigation revealed that the practice had no drug-free workplace policy, no random testing program, and no process for monitoring controlled substance inventories. Beyond the criminal charges against the nurse, CMS determined the practice had failed to maintain a safe environment for patient care. Reinstating Medicare participation took 18 months and cost the practice over $600,000 in lost revenue.

A drug-free workplace isn't just about policy compliance — it's about patient safety. Healthcare workers under the influence of drugs or alcohol pose a direct risk to patients, coworkers, and themselves.

The Drug-Free Workplace Act of 1988

The Drug-Free Workplace Act of 1988 applies to organizations that receive federal contracts of $100,000 or more, or any federal grant. Many healthcare practices are subject to this requirement because they participate in Medicare or Medicaid programs, which are federal payment programs.

Under the Act, covered employers must:

  • Publish a drug-free workplace policy statement and distribute it to all employees
  • Establish a drug-free awareness program that informs employees about the dangers of drug abuse, the employer's policy, available counseling and assistance, and the penalties for violations
  • Require employees to notify the employer of any criminal drug statute conviction for a violation occurring in the workplace
  • Notify the federal contracting or granting agency within 10 days of learning of an employee conviction
  • Take appropriate personnel action within 30 days of learning of a conviction

Employee notification requirements

Under a drug-free workplace policy, employees are required to notify the employer of any criminal drug statute conviction within 5 days of the conviction, if the violation occurred in the workplace. This notification triggers the employer's obligation to report to the federal agency and take appropriate action.

Patient safety first: An employee who reports to work under the influence of illegal drugs or alcohol must be immediately removed from patient care duties. The situation must be documented per practice policy. Do not allow the employee to continue patient care activities or drive home — arrange safe transportation.

Impaired employees — immediate response

When an employee appears to be under the influence of drugs or alcohol at work, the supervisor should:

  1. Remove the employee from patient care duties immediately
  2. Move to a private area and document specific observations (slurred speech, unsteady gait, smell of alcohol, etc.)
  3. Have a witness present for the conversation
  4. Arrange safe transportation home — do not allow the employee to drive
  5. Follow up per the practice's drug-free workplace policy and HR procedures

Employee Assistance Programs (EAP)

An Employee Assistance Program (EAP) provides confidential counseling and referral services for employees dealing with substance abuse, mental health issues, or other personal problems that may affect work performance. Key facts about EAPs:

  • EAP services are confidential — the employer does not receive details of the employee's counseling
  • EAPs provide assessment, short-term counseling, and referrals to specialized treatment programs
  • Using an EAP is voluntary and does not replace disciplinary action for policy violations
  • EAPs are not an investigative tool — they exist to support employees, not monitor them

Prescription medications in the workplace

Controlled substances that are legally prescribed by a physician may be used in the workplace if they do not impair the employee's ability to perform their duties safely. Key guidelines:

  • If a medication may affect alertness, coordination, or judgment, the employee should notify their supervisor or Human Resources (HR)
  • The practice may make reasonable accommodations (modified duties, adjusted schedule) while the employee is taking the medication
  • Prescription medications are NOT treated the same as illegal drugs under workplace policies
  • Employees are not required to disclose their specific diagnosis — only that a medication may affect their ability to perform job duties safely
Real scenario: A medical assistant is prescribed opioid pain medication after dental surgery. She notifies her supervisor that the medication may cause drowsiness. The supervisor assigns her to administrative duties for the week rather than clinical patient care. This is a reasonable accommodation — the employee disclosed the relevant safety concern, and the practice adjusted duties accordingly.

Key takeaways

  • The Drug-Free Workplace Act applies to organizations receiving federal contracts or grants, including many healthcare practices.
  • Employees must notify the employer of drug convictions within 5 days.
  • Impaired employees must be immediately removed from patient care duties and the situation documented.
  • EAPs provide confidential counseling and referral — they do not replace discipline or serve as investigation tools.
  • Prescription medications are permitted if they don't impair safe job performance; employees should notify supervisors of potential impairment.

You're ready for the quiz. Five questions on drug-free workplace policies. You need 80% to pass and earn your certificate.

quizQuestions.question: The Drug-Free Workplace Act of 1988 applies to:
options: All businesses in the United States
Only government agencies
Organizations that receive federal contracts or grants, including many healthcare practices
Only schools and universities
quizQuestions.explanation: The Drug-Free Workplace Act applies to organizations receiving federal contracts of $100,000+ or any federal grant. Many healthcare practices receiving Medicare or Medicaid payments are subject to this requirement.

quizQuestions.question: An employee who reports to work under the influence of illegal drugs or alcohol:
options: Should be allowed to work if they feel capable
Should be removed from patient care duties immediately and the situation documented per practice policy
Should be given coffee to sober up
Should only be addressed if a patient complains
quizQuestions.explanation: An impaired employee poses a direct risk to patient safety and must be immediately removed from duties. The incident should be documented and handled per the practice's drug-free workplace policy.

quizQuestions.question: Under a drug-free workplace policy, employees are generally required to:
options: Submit to random drug testing at any time without notice
Notify the employer of any criminal drug statute conviction within 5 days
Disclose all prescription medications they take
Abstain from all alcohol consumption including off-duty hours
quizQuestions.explanation: The Drug-Free Workplace Act requires employees to notify their employer of any criminal drug statute conviction for a violation occurring in the workplace no later than 5 days after the conviction.

quizQuestions.question: A practice's Employee Assistance Program (EAP) is designed to:
options: Replace disciplinary action for drug violations
Provide confidential counseling and referral services for employees dealing with substance abuse, mental health, or personal issues
Monitor employees' personal lives outside of work
Investigate employees suspected of drug use
quizQuestions.explanation: An EAP provides confidential assessment, counseling, and referral services to employees who may be dealing with substance abuse, mental health issues, or other personal problems that affect work performance.

quizQuestions.question: Controlled substances prescribed by a physician:
options: Are automatically acceptable in the workplace with no restrictions
May be used in the workplace if they do not impair the employee's ability to perform duties safely; the employee should notify their supervisor or HR if the medication may affect job performance
Must be disclosed to all coworkers
Are treated the same as illegal drugs under workplace policies
quizQuestions.explanation: Prescription medications are generally permitted, but employees must be able to perform their duties safely. If a medication may impair job performance, the employee should notify their supervisor or HR so appropriate accommodations can be made.

title: OSHA Recordkeeping & Reporting
description: Learn OSHA recordkeeping requirements including Forms 300, 300A, and 301, what qualifies as recordable, reporting deadlines for fatalities and severe injuries, annual posting requirements, and record retention rules.
lessonContent:
Key Terms
OSHA Form 300 = Log of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses
OSHA Form 300A = Summary of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses (annual posting)
OSHA Form 301 = Injury and Illness Incident Report
Recordable Injury/Illness = A work-related injury or illness that meets specific OSHA recording criteria
29 CFR 1904 = OSHA's recordkeeping regulation
First Aid = Treatment that does not trigger OSHA recordability (e.g., bandages, non-prescription medications at nonprescription strength)
MSD = Musculoskeletal Disorder
DART Rate = Days Away, Restricted, or Transferred rate — a key safety metric
NAICS Code = North American Industry Classification System code used to identify your industry

Why recordkeeping matters

OSHA recordkeeping is not paperwork for its own sake. Injury and illness records reveal patterns — which departments have the most injuries, which types of incidents keep recurring, and whether your safety interventions are working. Without accurate records, you are managing safety blind. And for healthcare practices, recordkeeping is not optional regardless of size.

This course covers who must keep OSHA records, which injuries and illnesses are recordable, how to complete the required forms, and the critical reporting timelines that carry severe penalties if missed.

Who must keep OSHA records

The default rule is that all employers with 11 or more employees at any point during the previous calendar year must maintain OSHA injury and illness records. Employers with 10 or fewer employees are exempt from routine recordkeeping (but not from reporting fatalities and severe injuries).

There is also an industry-based exemption for certain low-hazard industries. However, this exemption does not apply to healthcare. Under NAICS codes 621 (Ambulatory Health Care Services) and 622 (Hospitals), healthcare employers are always required to keep records, regardless of the low-hazard industry exemption list.

Healthcare is never exempt: Even if your medical practice has fewer than 11 employees, you must still report fatalities within 8 hours and hospitalizations, amputations, or losses of an eye within 24 hours. And if you have 11 or more employees, you must maintain the full OSHA 300, 300A, and 301 forms — no exceptions for healthcare.

The three OSHA recordkeeping forms

OSHA requires three forms for documenting workplace injuries and illnesses:

  • OSHA Form 300 — Log of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses: This is the running log. Each recordable injury or illness gets a line entry showing the employee name, job title, date of injury, where it happened, a description of the injury, and the outcome (days away from work, restricted duty, transfer, or other). The 300 Log is maintained throughout the year.
  • OSHA Form 300A — Summary of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses: At the end of each calendar year, you total the entries from the 300 Log and transfer the summary data to the 300A form. The 300A must be certified by a company executive and posted in a visible location at the workplace from February 1 through April 30 of the following year. All employees must be able to see it.
  • OSHA Form 301 — Injury and Illness Incident Report: This is the detailed report for each individual incident. It captures the specifics — what the employee was doing, what happened, what object or substance was involved, and the nature of the injury. You complete a Form 301 (or an equivalent form such as a state workers' compensation first report of injury) for every recordable case.

What qualifies as OSHA-recordable

Not every workplace injury requires OSHA recording. An injury or illness is recordable if it is work-related AND results in any of the following six outcomes:

  1. Death
  2. Days away from work — the employee misses one or more days beyond the day of injury
  3. Restricted work activity or job transfer — the employee cannot perform their normal duties
  4. Medical treatment beyond first aid — prescription medications, sutures, physical therapy, etc.
  5. Loss of consciousness
  6. A significant injury or illness diagnosed by a physician or other licensed healthcare professional — even without the other criteria (e.g., cancer, chronic irreversible disease, fractured bone, punctured eardrum)
First aid vs. medical treatment: A medical assistant gets a small cut while opening a supply package. The cut is cleaned and a bandage is applied. That is first aid — not recordable. But if the same cut requires sutures, it becomes medical treatment beyond first aid — and it is recordable. The distinction between first aid and medical treatment is the most common source of recordkeeping errors.

The 7-day logging deadline

Once you determine that an injury or illness is recordable, you have 7 calendar days to enter it on the OSHA Form 300 Log and complete the OSHA Form 301 Incident Report. This clock starts when you learn about the recordable case — not necessarily when the injury occurred. If an employee reports a back injury on Friday and you determine Monday that it requires restricted duty, you have 7 days from Monday.

Fatality and severe injury reporting

These are separate from the recordkeeping forms and have much shorter deadlines. Every employer — regardless of size or industry — must report:

  • Within 8 hours: Any work-related fatality. The 8-hour clock starts when the employer learns of the death (not when the death occurs). If a work-related injury results in death within 30 days of the incident, it must be reported.
  • Within 24 hours: Any work-related in-patient hospitalization, amputation, or loss of an eye. The 24-hour clock starts when the employer learns of the event.

Reports can be made by calling OSHA's toll-free number (1-800-321-OSHA), calling your local OSHA area office, or using OSHA's online reporting portal. Failure to report within these timeframes can result in citations and significant penalties.

Common mistake: Confusing "hospitalization" with "emergency room visit." An employee who goes to the ER, gets treated, and goes home was not hospitalized — that is an ER visit. In-patient hospitalization means the employee is formally admitted to the hospital. Only in-patient hospitalization triggers the 24-hour reporting requirement.

The 300A annual posting requirement

The OSHA Form 300A must be posted in a conspicuous place where employee notices are customarily posted from February 1 through April 30 of the year following the covered year. For example, the 2025 summary must be posted from February 1, 2026 through April 30, 2026.

The 300A must be certified by a company executive — an owner, officer, or the highest-ranking official at the establishment. This is not a task that can be delegated to a clerk without executive review and signature.

Record retention

OSHA requires that you retain the Form 300 Log, the annual 300A Summary, and the Form 301 Incident Reports for 5 years following the end of the calendar year they cover. During that retention period, you must also update the stored Form 300 Log if you discover that a previously recorded case has changed — for example, if an employee initially on restricted duty later requires days away from work.

Employee access to records

Employees have the right to review the OSHA Form 300 Log and the Form 301 Incident Report for their own injuries. Employee representatives (such as union representatives) have the right to review the Form 300 Log. The 300A Summary is public by nature — it is posted for all employees to see.

Electronic reporting requirements

Establishments with 250 or more employees, and establishments with 20-249 employees in certain high-hazard industries (including healthcare), must electronically submit injury and illness data to OSHA annually. This data is submitted through OSHA's Injury Tracking Application (ITA). OSHA publishes some of this data publicly to promote transparency and informed decision-making.

Key takeaways

  • Healthcare employers with 11 or more employees must maintain OSHA Forms 300, 300A, and 301. Healthcare is never exempt from the industry-based exemption.
  • An injury is recordable if it is work-related and results in death, days away, restricted duty, medical treatment beyond first aid, loss of consciousness, or significant diagnosis.
  • Recordable cases must be logged within 7 calendar days of learning about the case.
  • Fatalities must be reported within 8 hours. Hospitalizations, amputations, and eye losses within 24 hours.
  • The Form 300A must be posted February 1 through April 30 each year, certified by a company executive.
  • Records must be retained for 5 years and updated if case outcomes change.

Ready for the quiz. Ten questions. You need 80% to pass.

quizQuestions.question: Which employers are required to maintain OSHA injury and illness records (Forms 300, 300A, and 301)?
options: Only employers with 50 or more employees
All employers with 11 or more employees, with no exemption for healthcare regardless of the low-hazard industry list
Only hospitals — medical offices and clinics are exempt
Only employers that have had a workplace injury in the past year
quizQuestions.explanation: Employers with 11 or more employees must maintain OSHA records. Healthcare employers (NAICS codes 621 and 622) are always required to keep records and are never exempt under the low-hazard industry exemption.

quizQuestions.question: A work-related injury is OSHA-recordable if it results in any of the following EXCEPT:
options: Days away from work
Medical treatment beyond first aid (e.g., sutures, prescription medication)
First aid treatment only (e.g., a bandage or non-prescription medication at nonprescription strength)
Loss of consciousness
quizQuestions.explanation: Injuries requiring only first aid — bandages, non-prescription medications at nonprescription strength, tetanus shots, wound cleaning — are not OSHA-recordable. The six recordable criteria are: death, days away from work, restricted duty/transfer, medical treatment beyond first aid, loss of consciousness, and significant diagnosis by a licensed healthcare professional.

quizQuestions.question: How quickly must a work-related fatality be reported to OSHA?
options: Within 24 hours
Within 7 calendar days
Within 8 hours of the employer learning of the fatality
By the end of the next business day
quizQuestions.explanation: Work-related fatalities must be reported to OSHA within 8 hours of when the employer learns of the death. This applies to all employers regardless of size or industry.

quizQuestions.question: Work-related in-patient hospitalizations, amputations, and losses of an eye must be reported to OSHA within:
options: 8 hours
24 hours of the employer learning of the event
7 calendar days
30 calendar days
quizQuestions.explanation: In-patient hospitalizations, amputations, and losses of an eye must be reported within 24 hours of when the employer learns of the event. Note: an emergency room visit where the employee is treated and released is NOT an in-patient hospitalization.

quizQuestions.question: The OSHA Form 300A (Annual Summary) must be posted in the workplace during which period?
options: January 1 through March 31
February 1 through April 30
The entire calendar year
Only during OSHA inspections
quizQuestions.explanation: The OSHA Form 300A must be posted in a conspicuous location from February 1 through April 30 of the year following the covered year. It must be certified by a company executive.

quizQuestions.question: How long must OSHA recordkeeping forms (300, 300A, and 301) be retained?
options: 1 year after the end of the calendar year they cover
3 years after the end of the calendar year they cover
5 years following the end of the calendar year they cover
Indefinitely — they must never be destroyed
quizQuestions.explanation: OSHA requires retention of Forms 300, 300A, and 301 for 5 years following the end of the calendar year they cover. During that period, the Form 300 must be updated if previously recorded case outcomes change.

quizQuestions.question: Once an employer determines that an injury or illness is recordable, how many days do they have to enter it on the OSHA Form 300 Log?
options: 24 hours
3 business days
7 calendar days from when the employer learns the case is recordable
30 calendar days
quizQuestions.explanation: Recordable cases must be entered on the Form 300 Log and a Form 301 must be completed within 7 calendar days of when the employer learns about the recordable case.

quizQuestions.question: An employee goes to the emergency room after a workplace incident, receives treatment, and is sent home the same day. Is this considered an in-patient hospitalization for OSHA 24-hour reporting purposes?
options: Yes — any ER visit triggers the 24-hour reporting requirement
No — in-patient hospitalization means formal admission to the hospital, not an ER visit where the employee is treated and released
Only if the employee was transported by ambulance
Only if the employee missed their next scheduled shift
quizQuestions.explanation: An ER visit where the employee is treated and released is NOT an in-patient hospitalization. The 24-hour reporting requirement is triggered only by formal hospital admission (in-patient hospitalization), amputation, or loss of an eye.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the purpose of the OSHA Form 301?
options: It is the annual summary posted for employees to see
It is the running log of all injuries and illnesses during the year
It is the detailed incident report completed for each individual recordable case, capturing specifics of what happened
It is the form submitted to OSHA electronically each year
quizQuestions.explanation: OSHA Form 301 is the Injury and Illness Incident Report — a detailed report for each individual recordable case. It captures what the employee was doing, what happened, what object/substance was involved, and the nature of the injury. Form 300 is the log; Form 300A is the annual summary.

quizQuestions.question: A medical office with 8 employees has a work-related fatality. Which statement is correct?
options: The office is exempt from all OSHA recordkeeping and reporting because it has fewer than 11 employees
The office does not need to keep Forms 300/300A/301, but it MUST report the fatality to OSHA within 8 hours
The office only needs to report the fatality if OSHA specifically requests information
Small medical offices are completely exempt from OSHA requirements
quizQuestions.explanation: While employers with 10 or fewer employees are exempt from routine OSHA recordkeeping (Forms 300, 300A, 301), ALL employers must report fatalities within 8 hours and hospitalizations, amputations, or losses of an eye within 24 hours. There is no size exemption for severe injury reporting.

title: Electrical Safety in Healthcare
description: Learn to identify and control electrical hazards in medical offices, including GFCI requirements, extension cord safety, medical equipment protocols, lockout/tagout basics, and OSHA 29 CFR 1910 Subpart S requirements.
lessonContent:
Key Terms
GFCI = Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter — a device that shuts off electric power when it detects current flowing along an unintended path
Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) = Safety procedure to ensure that dangerous equipment is properly shut off and cannot be started again before maintenance is complete
29 CFR 1910 Subpart S = OSHA's general industry standard for electrical safety
Arc Flash = A dangerous electrical explosion caused by a fault condition
Ground Fault = An unintended electrical path between a power source and a grounded surface
Surge Protector = A device that protects electronic equipment from voltage spikes
UL Listed = Certified by Underwriters Laboratories as meeting safety standards
NFPA 70E = National Fire Protection Association standard for electrical safety in the workplace

Electrical hazards are real in medical offices

Medical offices rely on a wide range of electrical equipment — from computers and printers to diagnostic machines, autoclaves, and patient monitoring devices. The combination of aging building wiring, high equipment density, and the presence of water and conductive materials in clinical areas creates electrical hazard conditions that are often overlooked in healthcare settings.

Electrocution is consistently among the top causes of workplace fatalities tracked by OSHA. While fatal electrocutions are more common in construction, non-fatal electrical injuries including shocks and burns occur regularly in healthcare facilities. This course covers the specific electrical hazards you may encounter and the practical steps to eliminate or control them.

Common electrical hazards in medical offices

Healthcare facilities present several specific electrical hazard scenarios:

  • Overloaded circuits: Medical offices often add equipment over time without upgrading electrical capacity. Multiple high-draw devices on a single circuit can cause overheating, tripped breakers, and fire risk.
  • Damaged cords and plugs: Frayed, cracked, or pinched power cords are a direct shock and fire hazard. Equipment cords that run under furniture, through doorways, or across walkways are subject to constant damage.
  • Wet locations: Exam rooms, labs, sterilization areas, and break rooms with sinks create conditions where electrical shock risk is elevated. Water is a conductor — any electrical equipment near water sources must have appropriate protection.
  • Improper grounding: Medical equipment must be properly grounded. A three-prong plug with the grounding pin broken off, or a two-prong adapter used on a three-prong device, eliminates the grounding protection that prevents shock.
  • Space heaters and personal appliances: Unauthorized personal appliances are a leading cause of electrical fires in offices. Space heaters draw significant current and are frequently placed near combustible materials.
Never remove the grounding pin: The third prong on a plug is not optional — it is a safety device that provides a path to ground for fault current. Removing or bypassing it means that in a ground fault, the current flows through you instead of safely to ground. This is one of the most common and most dangerous electrical safety violations.

Ground Fault Circuit Interrupters (GFCIs)

A GFCI monitors the current flowing through a circuit and shuts off power within milliseconds if it detects a difference between the outgoing and returning current — which indicates current is flowing through an unintended path (such as a person). GFCIs are critical in wet locations.

  • Where GFCIs are required: OSHA and the National Electrical Code require GFCI protection in wet or damp locations, bathrooms, kitchens, areas near sinks, and outdoor receptacles. In healthcare, this includes exam rooms with sinks, lab areas, sterilization rooms, and break rooms.
  • Testing GFCIs: GFCIs should be tested monthly using the test/reset buttons on the device. Press the "Test" button — the power should shut off. Press "Reset" to restore power. If the GFCI fails to trip when tested, it must be replaced immediately.
  • GFCI types: GFCIs can be installed as receptacle outlets (the type with test/reset buttons built into the outlet), circuit breakers (installed in the electrical panel), or portable devices (plug-in units used with extension cords).

Extension cord safety

Extension cords are intended for temporary use. In healthcare settings, they are frequently misused as permanent wiring, creating significant hazards:

  • Extension cords are not permanent wiring. OSHA prohibits the use of extension cords as a substitute for fixed wiring. If you need more outlets, have an electrician install additional receptacles.
  • Never daisy-chain extension cords. Plugging one extension cord into another increases resistance, generates heat, and creates a fire hazard.
  • Match the cord to the load. An extension cord must be rated for the current draw of the equipment it powers. Using a light-duty household extension cord for a medical device that draws significant current is a fire hazard.
  • Do not run cords through walls, ceilings, or doorways. Cords passing through walls or above drop ceilings are concealed and cannot be inspected for damage. Cords through doorways are subject to pinching and crushing.
  • Inspect cords before each use. Check for cracked insulation, exposed wires, bent prongs, and damage to the outer jacket. Damaged cords must be taken out of service immediately — not repaired with tape.
Scenario: A practice manager notices that the front desk area has three power strips daisy-chained together, powering two computers, three monitors, a printer, a phone charger, and a desk lamp. This is an overloaded circuit and a fire waiting to happen. The correct action is to have an electrician install additional outlets and distribute the load across multiple circuits.

Medical equipment electrical safety

Medical equipment has additional electrical safety considerations beyond standard office equipment:

  • Use only equipment that is UL Listed or equivalent. Certification marks indicate the device has been tested and meets safety standards.
  • Follow manufacturer maintenance schedules. Medical equipment should be inspected and maintained on the schedule specified by the manufacturer. This includes electrical safety testing (leakage current testing) for devices that contact patients.
  • Do not modify equipment. Altering wiring, bypassing safety interlocks, or making unauthorized repairs to medical equipment creates electrical hazards and violates manufacturer warranties and potentially FDA regulations.
  • Report malfunctions immediately. Any equipment that gives a shock (even a mild tingle), produces a burning smell, makes unusual sounds, or shows visible damage to wiring or housing must be taken out of service and reported.
  • Biomedical engineering inspection: Patient-contact electrical equipment should receive periodic biomedical safety inspections to verify grounding integrity and leakage current limits.

Lockout/Tagout basics for healthcare

Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) procedures ensure that equipment is completely de-energized and cannot be accidentally restarted while being serviced or maintained. While LOTO is most associated with industrial settings, it applies in healthcare whenever employees service or maintain electrically powered equipment.

  • When LOTO applies: Any time an employee must access the internal components of electrically powered equipment for maintenance, cleaning, or repair where unexpected energization could cause injury.
  • Basic LOTO steps: (1) Notify affected employees, (2) Shut down the equipment using normal stopping procedure, (3) Isolate the energy source (unplug or turn off the circuit breaker), (4) Apply the lock and tag, (5) Verify that the equipment is de-energized by attempting to start it, (6) Perform the maintenance, (7) Remove the lock and tag only after maintenance is complete and all personnel are clear.
  • Only the person who applied the lock may remove it. This is a fundamental LOTO rule. No supervisor, manager, or coworker may remove another person's lock.

Surge protection for sensitive equipment

Healthcare facilities contain expensive and sensitive electronic equipment that can be damaged or destroyed by power surges — sudden increases in voltage caused by lightning, utility switching, or large equipment cycling on and off:

  • Use surge protectors for all sensitive electronic equipment. Computers, servers, diagnostic equipment, and electronic health record (EHR) workstations should be connected through surge protectors rated for the equipment's power requirements.
  • Surge protectors are not power strips. A basic power strip provides additional outlets but no surge protection. Look for a UL 1449 listing and a joule rating appropriate for the equipment.
  • Replace surge protectors after a major surge event. Surge protectors degrade after absorbing surges. After a significant power event (lightning strike, major outage), replace surge protectors even if they appear to be functioning.
  • Consider uninterruptible power supplies (UPS). For critical systems like EHR servers, a UPS provides both surge protection and battery backup during power outages, preventing data loss and allowing graceful shutdown.

Reporting electrical hazards

Every employee has a responsibility to identify and report electrical hazards. Do not attempt to repair electrical problems yourself unless you are a qualified electrician:

  • Report sparking outlets, flickering lights, burning smells, warm outlet covers, or tripping breakers immediately.
  • Report damaged cords, broken plugs, missing grounding pins, or cracked outlet covers.
  • If you receive a shock from any equipment or outlet, report it immediately — even if the shock seems minor. A minor shock indicates a fault that could be fatal to the next person.
  • Do not use equipment that has been reported as faulty until it has been inspected and cleared by a qualified person. Tag it "Out of Service" to prevent others from using it.

OSHA 29 CFR 1910 Subpart S

OSHA's general industry electrical safety standards (29 CFR 1910 Subpart S) apply to all healthcare facilities. Key requirements include:

  • Electrical equipment must be free from recognized hazards likely to cause death or serious physical harm.
  • Listed or labeled equipment must be used in accordance with the instructions included in the listing or labeling.
  • Workspace around electrical panels must be kept clear — at least 3 feet of clearance in front of electrical panels at all times.
  • Employees must be trained on electrical safety hazards relevant to their work.
  • Only qualified persons may work on energized electrical equipment.

Key takeaways

  • Overloaded circuits, damaged cords, improper grounding, and wet locations are the most common electrical hazards in healthcare settings.
  • GFCIs are required in wet locations and must be tested monthly.
  • Extension cords are for temporary use only — never as permanent wiring, never daisy-chained.
  • Never remove or bypass the grounding pin on a three-prong plug.
  • Medical equipment must be UL Listed, maintained per manufacturer specifications, and taken out of service immediately if any electrical fault is detected.
  • Report all electrical hazards immediately. Do not attempt repairs unless you are a qualified electrician.
  • OSHA 29 CFR 1910 Subpart S governs electrical safety in healthcare facilities.

Ready for the quiz. Eight questions. You need 80% to pass.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary function of a Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter (GFCI)?
options: To prevent power surges from damaging equipment
To detect current flowing through an unintended path (such as a person) and shut off power within milliseconds
To increase the electrical capacity of a circuit
To provide backup power during an outage
quizQuestions.explanation: A GFCI monitors current flow and shuts off power within milliseconds if it detects a difference between outgoing and returning current, which indicates current is flowing through an unintended path — potentially through a person. GFCIs are critical in wet locations.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a violation of OSHA extension cord safety rules?
options: Using a heavy-duty extension cord temporarily while an outlet is being repaired
Using extension cords as permanent wiring substitutes and daisy-chaining multiple cords together
Inspecting extension cords before each use
Using a three-prong extension cord with a grounding pin intact
quizQuestions.explanation: OSHA prohibits the use of extension cords as a substitute for fixed wiring. Daisy-chaining (plugging one extension cord into another) increases resistance, generates heat, and creates a fire hazard. Extension cords are for temporary use only.

quizQuestions.question: How often should GFCIs be tested using the test/reset buttons?
options: Once a year during annual safety inspection
Only when they appear to malfunction
Monthly
Every time equipment is plugged in
quizQuestions.explanation: GFCIs should be tested monthly using the test/reset buttons. Press the Test button — power should shut off. Press Reset to restore power. If the GFCI fails to trip when tested, it must be replaced immediately.

quizQuestions.question: Why is it dangerous to remove or bypass the grounding pin (third prong) on a three-prong plug?
options: It reduces the power available to the equipment
It voids the manufacturer warranty but has no safety impact
Without grounding, fault current flows through the person touching the equipment instead of safely to ground
It causes the circuit breaker to trip more frequently
quizQuestions.explanation: The grounding pin provides a safe path to ground for fault current. Without it, in a ground fault condition, the electrical current flows through the person touching the equipment instead of safely to ground. This is one of the most common and most dangerous electrical safety violations.

quizQuestions.question: During a lockout/tagout (LOTO) procedure, who is authorized to remove the lock?
options: Any supervisor or manager
The facility maintenance director
Only the person who applied the lock
Anyone who has completed LOTO training
quizQuestions.explanation: Only the person who applied the lock may remove it. This is a fundamental LOTO rule that prevents accidental re-energization while someone is still working on the equipment. No supervisor, manager, or coworker may remove another person's lock.

quizQuestions.question: A staff member receives a mild electrical tingle when touching a piece of medical equipment. What is the correct response?
options: Ignore it — mild shocks from medical equipment are normal
Wrap the cord with electrical tape and continue using the equipment
Take the equipment out of service immediately, tag it 'Out of Service,' and report the incident
Switch to a different outlet and continue using the equipment
quizQuestions.explanation: Any shock — even a mild tingle — indicates an electrical fault that could be fatal to the next person who touches the equipment. The equipment must be taken out of service immediately, tagged, and reported. Never attempt to repair it yourself unless you are a qualified electrician.

quizQuestions.question: What is the minimum clearance that must be maintained in front of electrical panels per OSHA standards?
options: 1 foot
2 feet
3 feet
No clearance is required as long as the panel door can open
quizQuestions.explanation: OSHA requires at least 3 feet of clearance in front of electrical panels at all times. This space is necessary for safe access during maintenance and for emergency response to electrical issues. Storage, furniture, and equipment must not block this clearance.

quizQuestions.question: What is the difference between a surge protector and a basic power strip?
options: There is no difference — they are the same product with different names
A surge protector provides voltage spike protection (look for UL 1449 listing and joule rating), while a basic power strip only provides additional outlets with no surge protection
A power strip provides more outlets than a surge protector
A surge protector is only needed for computers, not for medical equipment
quizQuestions.explanation: A basic power strip provides additional outlets but no protection against voltage spikes. A surge protector includes circuitry that diverts excess voltage, protecting sensitive equipment. Look for a UL 1449 listing and a joule rating appropriate for the equipment being protected.

title: USP 797 Allergen Extract Compounding
description: Covers the requirements of USP General Chapter 797 §21 for allergen extract compounding: aseptic technique, beyond-use dating, proper labeling, storage conditions, and documentation. Required for all staff who prepare or handle compounded allergen extracts.
lessonContent: Lesson content coming soon.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary purpose of USP 797 §21?
options: To regulate the pricing of allergen extracts
To establish standards for compounding sterile preparations including allergen extracts
To define insurance coverage for allergy testing
To set guidelines for allergy diagnosis procedures
quizQuestions.explanation: USP 797 §21 establishes minimum standards for compounding sterile preparations, including allergen extracts, to prevent harm from contamination, incorrect ingredients, or improper preparation.

quizQuestions.question: How many components make up the USP 797 §21 annual competency requirement?
options: One (written quiz only)
Two (written quiz and media fill)
Three (written quiz, gloved fingertip sampling, and media fill)
Four (written quiz, observation, fingertip sampling, and media fill)
quizQuestions.explanation: USP 797 §21 requires three competency components annually: a written knowledge assessment, gloved fingertip and thumb sampling (aseptic technique verification), and a media fill test (simulated compounding).

quizQuestions.question: What is the maximum beyond-use date (BUD) for a compounded allergen extract mixed from FDA-approved concentrates?
options: 24 hours
48 hours
The manufacturer's expiration date of the shortest-dated component
One year from compounding
quizQuestions.explanation: When mixing FDA-approved allergen extract concentrates, the BUD is the earliest expiration date among the component vials, as the compounded product cannot exceed the stability of its shortest-dated ingredient.

quizQuestions.question: At what temperature range must allergen extracts be stored?
options: Room temperature (20–25°C / 68–77°F)
Refrigerated (2–8°C / 36–46°F)
Frozen (−20°C / −4°F)
Any temperature below 30°C / 86°F
quizQuestions.explanation: Allergen extracts must be stored refrigerated at 2–8°C (36–46°F) to maintain potency. Temperature excursions must be documented, and extracts exposed to temperatures outside this range may need to be discarded.

quizQuestions.question: How often should the emergency epinephrine kit be inspected in an allergy practice?
options: Annually
Quarterly
Monthly — checking expiration dates, presence of all items, and functionality
Only when an emergency occurs
quizQuestions.explanation: Emergency kits should be inspected at least monthly. Each inspection should verify all items are present, medications are not expired, and equipment (auto-injectors, airways) is functional. The inspection must be documented with the inspector's signature and date.

quizQuestions.question: What is the purpose of gloved fingertip and thumb sampling?
options: To test the potency of allergen extracts
To verify that the compounder maintains proper aseptic technique during handling
To check for latex allergies in staff
To measure hand steadiness for injection procedures
quizQuestions.explanation: Gloved fingertip and thumb sampling tests the compounder's ability to maintain aseptic technique. Fingertips and thumbs are pressed onto growth media after performing hand hygiene and gloving; any microbial growth indicates a break in aseptic technique.

quizQuestions.question: How many successful gloved fingertip sampling passes are required for initial competency?
options: One pass
Two passes
Three consecutive passes with zero colony-forming units
Five passes
quizQuestions.explanation: Initial competency requires three consecutive successful passes (zero colony-forming units on each). Annual renewal requires only one successful pass, as the compounder has already demonstrated baseline competency.

quizQuestions.question: What should happen if a staff member's media fill test shows contamination?
options: Ignore it if the contamination level is low
The staff member must stop compounding, receive remedial training, and pass a repeat media fill before resuming
Document the failure and schedule a retest in 6 months
Switch to a different brand of growth media
quizQuestions.explanation: A failed media fill indicates a break in aseptic technique. The compounder must immediately stop compounding activities, undergo remedial training in aseptic technique, and demonstrate competency with a successful repeat media fill before being allowed to resume compounding.

title: Anaphylaxis Emergency Response
description: Training on recognition, immediate treatment, and documentation of anaphylactic reactions in the clinical setting. Covers epinephrine administration, monitoring protocols, emergency kit management, and post-event reporting. Required for all clinical staff in allergy practices.
lessonContent: Lesson content coming soon.

quizQuestions.question: What is the FIRST medication that should be administered when anaphylaxis is suspected?
options: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) IV
Intramuscular epinephrine (1:1000) in the mid-outer thigh
Albuterol nebulizer
IV corticosteroids
quizQuestions.explanation: Intramuscular epinephrine (1:1000 concentration) injected into the mid-outer thigh is the FIRST-LINE treatment for anaphylaxis. Delays in epinephrine administration are associated with fatal outcomes. All other medications are adjunctive and should not delay epinephrine.

quizQuestions.question: What is the standard adult dose of epinephrine for anaphylaxis?
options: 0.01 mg IM
0.15 mg IM
0.3–0.5 mg IM (1:1000 concentration)
1.0 mg IV push
quizQuestions.explanation: The standard adult dose is 0.3–0.5 mg of 1:1000 (1 mg/mL) epinephrine administered intramuscularly. The dose may be repeated every 5–15 minutes if symptoms do not improve. Pediatric dosing is 0.01 mg/kg up to 0.3 mg.

quizQuestions.question: How long should a patient be monitored after an anaphylactic reaction is treated?
options: 15 minutes
30 minutes
At least 60 minutes, with consideration for 4–6 hours for severe reactions (biphasic risk)
Monitoring is optional if symptoms resolve
quizQuestions.explanation: Patients should be monitored for at least 60 minutes after symptom resolution. Severe reactions warrant 4–6 hours of observation due to the risk of biphasic anaphylaxis (recurrence of symptoms hours after initial resolution). Up to 20% of anaphylaxis cases are biphasic.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT a common early sign of anaphylaxis?
options: Urticaria (hives) and flushing
Throat tightness or difficulty swallowing
Isolated mild headache without other symptoms
Hypotension with rapid pulse
quizQuestions.explanation: Anaphylaxis typically presents with skin symptoms (hives, flushing), respiratory compromise (throat tightening, wheezing), and/or cardiovascular collapse (hypotension, tachycardia). An isolated mild headache without skin, respiratory, or cardiovascular symptoms is not typical of anaphylaxis.

quizQuestions.question: How often should anaphylaxis emergency drills be conducted in an allergy practice?
options: Once when the practice opens
Annually
At least twice yearly (every 6 months), with all clinical staff participating
Only after an actual anaphylaxis event
quizQuestions.explanation: Best practice guidelines recommend conducting anaphylaxis emergency drills at least twice yearly. All clinical staff should participate, and drills should simulate realistic scenarios including epinephrine administration, patient positioning, vital sign monitoring, and EMS activation.

quizQuestions.question: What position should a patient experiencing anaphylaxis with hypotension be placed in?
options: Sitting upright
Supine (lying flat) with legs elevated, unless contraindicated by respiratory distress or vomiting
Left lateral decubitus (recovery position)
Standing to maintain consciousness
quizQuestions.explanation: Patients with anaphylaxis and hypotension should be placed supine with legs elevated to improve venous return. Exception: patients with significant respiratory distress may need to sit partially upright, and vomiting patients should be placed in recovery position to prevent aspiration.
